{{Short description|none}} <!-- "none" is preferred when the title is sufficiently descriptive; see [[WP:SDNONE]] -->
{{Use American English|date=February 2025}}
{{Use mdy dates|date=June 2021}}
{{Infobox event
 |title=Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in the United States
 |type=[[Global recession]]
 |image=IMF World Economic Outlook January 2021 Real GDP growth rate (map).svg
 |caption=Map showing [[List of countries by real GDP growth rate|real GDP growth rates]] in 2020, as projected by the [[International Monetary Fund]]
 |cause=[[COVID-19 pandemic]]–induced [[2020 stock market crash|stock market crash]] and lockdown
 |date=February 2020–May 2023<ref>{{cite web|title=Gross Domestic Product, 4th Quarter and Year 2020 (Advance Estimate)|date=January 28, 2021|publisher=[[Bureau of Economic Analysis]]|url=https://www.bea.gov/news/2021/gross-domestic-product-4th-quarter-and-year-2020-advance-estimate|access-date=May 1, 2021}}</ref>
 |outcome=
* Sharp rise in unemployment
* Stress on supply chains
* Decrease in government income
* Collapse of the travel, tourism, and [[hospitality industry|hospitality industries]]
* Reduced consumer activity
}}
{{COVID-19 pandemic sidebar}}
The '''economic impact of the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States]]''' has been widely disruptive, adversely affecting travel, financial markets, employment, shipping, and other industries. The impacts can be attributed not just to government intervention to contain the virus (including at the federal and state level), but also to consumer and business behavior to reduce exposure to and spread of the deadly virus.

Real GDP contracted in 2020 by 3.5%, the first contraction since the [[2008 financial crisis]]. Millions of workers were dislocated from their jobs, leading to multiple weeks of record shattering numbers of unemployment insurance applications. Consumer and retail activity contracted, with many businesses (especially restaurants) closing. Many businesses and offices transitioned to [[remote work]] to avoid the spread of COVID-19 at the office. Congress passed several pieces of legislation, such as the [[American Rescue Plan Act of 2021]] to provide [[Stimulus (economics)|stimulus]] to mitigate the effect of workplace closures and income losses. The [[Federal Reserve]] reduced the [[federal funds rate]] target to nearly zero and introduced several liquidity facilities to keep financial markets functioning and to provide stimulus. In late 2021, inflation began to increase to levels not seen since the 1980s. 

Recovery from the recession began relatively quickly, with the recession only lasting one quarter according to the NBER. As of 2022, the unemployment rate reached its pre-pandemic levels - nevertheless, in many key aspects and industries, the U.S. economy has not completely recovered from the COVID-19 pandemic.

A growing [[Digital divide|digital gap]] emerged in the United States following the pandemic, despite non-digital enterprises being more dynamic than in the [[European Union]]. In the United States, 48% of enterprises that were non-digital before to the pandemic began investing in digital technologies. 64% of firms that had previously implemented advanced digital technology also increased their investment in digitalisation.<ref name="Bank-2022b">{{Cite book |last=Bank |first=European Investment |url=https://www.eib.org/en/publications/digitalisation-in-europe-2021-2022 |title=Digitalisation in Europe 2021-2022: Evidence from the EIB Investment Survey |date=2022-05-05 |publisher=European Investment Bank |isbn=978-92-861-5233-7 |language=EN}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book |last1=European Investment Bank |url=https://data.europa.eu/doi/10.2867/222528 |title=The growing digital divide in Europe and the United States |last2=Rückert |first2=Désirée |last3=Veugelers |first3=Reinhilde |last4=Weiss |first4=Christoph |date=2020 |publisher=Publications Office of the European Union |isbn=978-92-861-4683-1 |location=LU|doi=10.2867/222528 }}</ref> In the United States, 20% of jobs were found within firms that have not digitally transformed. According to a recent survey, these are called "sleepwalking firms", and are also more likely to pay lower wages and to create lower employment. These firms were also less likely to train their employees throughout the COVID-19 outbreak.<ref name="Bank-2022c">{{Cite book |last=Bank |first=European Investment |url=https://www.eib.org/en/publications/investment-report-2021 |title=EIB Investment Report 2021/2022: Recovery as a springboard for change |date=2022-01-12 |publisher=European Investment Bank |isbn=978-92-861-5155-2 |language=EN}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=The recovery will be digital |url=https://www.mckinsey.com/~/media/mckinsey/business%20functions/mckinsey%20digital/our%20insights/how%20six%20companies%20are%20using%20technology%20and%20data%20to%20transform%20themselves/the-next-normal-the-recovery-will-be-digital.pdf}}</ref>

==Overview==
In 2020, the U.S. GDP contracted at a 3.5% annualized rate. It was the biggest contraction since 1946 and the first contraction since 2009.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2021|title=Gross Domestic Product, (Third Estimate), GDP by Industry, and Corporate Profits, Fourth Quarter and Year 2020|url=https://www.bea.gov/news/2021/gross-domestic-product-third-estimate-gdp-industry-and-corporate-profits-4th-quarter-and|access-date=2021-09-29|website=www.bea.gov}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|last=McCormick|first=Emily|date=January 28, 2021|title=4Q GDP: Economy expands at a 4.0% annualized rate|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/4q-gdp-2020-us-economy-coronavirus-pandemic-180133456.html|access-date=January 28, 2021|website=Yahoo Finance|language=en-US}}</ref>

The [[United States Census Bureau]]'s Household Pulse Survey published weekly statistics of the effects of the pandemic on Americans' lives. For week 12 (July 16–21), 51.1% of respondents reported a loss of employment income since March 13, 2020, 12.1% reported food scarcity, 40.1% delayed getting medical care in the past four weeks, and 26.5% reported [[Housing insecurity in the United States|housing insecurity]].<ref>{{cite web|author=<!--Not stated-->|date=July 21, 2020|title=Household Pulse Survey|url=https://www.census.gov/data-tools/demo/hhp/#/?measures=HIR|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200813194858/https://www.census.gov/data-tools/demo/hhp/#/?measures=HIR|archive-date=August 13, 2020|access-date=August 14, 2020|website=census.gov|publisher=[[United States Census Bureau]]}}</ref>

===Statistical summary===
The following table illustrates the impact of the pandemic on key economic measures. February 2020 represented the pre-crisis level for most variables, with the S&P 500 stock market index (a leading indicator) falling from its February 19 peak. From February through June, the number of persons with jobs was down 14.6&nbsp;million. The U.S. also added $3.1&nbsp;trillion to the public debt in just 4 months.

In October 2021, however, annual consumer price inflation reached 6.2%, the biggest rise in 31 years.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Horowitz|first=Julia|date=11 November 2021|title=Surging inflation sets up high-stakes fight in Washington|url=https://www.cnn.com/2021/11/11/investing/premarket-stocks-trading/index.html|access-date=2021-11-11|website=CNN}}</ref>

== Impact on individuals ==

=== Employment effects ===

{{See also|Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic|Racial disparities in the COVID-19 pandemic in the United States}}

==== Government statistics ====
[[File:US payrolls 2005 to 2022.png|thumb|upright=1.5|US non-farm payrolls, 2005 – January 2022]]
In the February 2020 jobs report, which reflected the employment situation before lockdowns began, 1.28&nbsp;million Americans were classed as "permanent job losers". In August, that number was at 3.41&nbsp;million, and in September it rose to 3.75&nbsp;million. "Temporary layoffs", meanwhile, decreased, suggesting that some job losses originally understood as "temporary" had now become permanent.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Ro|first=Sam|date=October 5, 2020|title=The economy's temporary problems are becoming permanent: Morning Brief|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/economy-temporary-layoff-permanent-job-loss-morning-brief-095426627.html|access-date=October 5, 2020|publisher=Yahoo! Finance|language=en-US}}</ref>

On May 8, 2020, the [[Bureau of Labor Statistics]] reported that 20.5&nbsp;million nonfarm jobs were lost and the unemployment rate rose to 14.7 percent in April.<ref name="bls-april-2020">{{cite press release|author=<!--Staff writer(s); no by-line.-->|date=May 8, 2020|title=THE EMPLOYMENT SITUATION – APRIL 2020|url=https://www.bls.gov/news.release/empsit.nr0.htm|url-status=live|publisher=bls.gov|agency=[[Bureau of Labor Statistics]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200510142714/https://www.bls.gov/news.release/empsit.nr0.htm|archive-date=May 10, 2020|access-date=May 10, 2020}}</ref> This followed reports of weekly initial claims for unemployment insurance that increased from a typical level of around 200,000 per week through early March, to 3.3&nbsp;million the week of March 21, a peak of 6.9&nbsp;million (March 28), and declines each week thereafter to 3.0&nbsp;million the week of May 9. A total of 36.5&nbsp;million filed for unemployment insurance from March 21 to May 9.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Cox|first1=Jeff|date=May 14, 2020|title=Weekly jobless claims total 2.981 million, bringing coronavirus tally to 36.5 million|publisher=CNBC|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/05/14/weekly-jobless-claims.html|access-date=May 17, 2020}}</ref> The [[Congressional Budget Office]] estimated that costs for unemployment insurance claims were $49&nbsp;billion in April 2020, versus $3&nbsp;billion in April 2019. An estimated $27&nbsp;billion of the increase was due to the $600/week increase in unemployment benefits due to the CARES Act.<ref>{{cite news|date=May 8, 2020|title=Monthly Budget Review for April 2020|work=urban.org|url=https://www.cbo.gov/publication/56350|access-date=May 15, 2020}}</ref>

On May 20, 2021, the Labor Department reported that there had been only 444,000 unemployment claims during the previous week, the lowest number since the beginning of the pandemic.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Rugaber|first=Christopher|date=May 20, 2021|title=U.S. Jobless Claims Decline To 444,000, A New Pandemic Low|url=https://www.huffpost.com/entry/jobless-claims-pandemic_n_60a6588fe4b0a256830f6797|access-date=May 20, 2021|website=HuffPost|language=en}}</ref>

The United States depended on direct payments and loans to help individuals and businesses, regardless of whether jobs were retained. As a result, while aggregate hours worked decreased by around 15% in the United States and the European Union, unemployment increased significantly in the United States. However, in the United States, most firms that implemented digital technology were better able to avert worker reductions than non-digital firms.<ref name="Bank-2022d">{{Cite book |last=Bank |first=European Investment |url=https://www.eib.org/en/publications/digitalisation-in-europe-2021-2022 |title=Digitalisation in Europe 2021-2022: Evidence from the EIB Investment Survey |date=2022-05-05 |publisher=European Investment Bank |isbn=978-92-861-5233-7 |language=EN}}</ref><ref name="OECD-2022">{{Cite web |title=Coronavirus (COVID-19): SME policy responses |url=https://www.oecd.org/coronavirus/policy-responses/coronavirus-covid-19-sme-policy-responses-04440101/ |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=OECD |language=en}}</ref><ref name="Giupponi-2020">{{Cite web |last1=Giupponi |first1=Giulia |last2=Landais |first2=Camille |date=2020-04-01 |title=Building effective short-time work schemes for the COVID-19 crisis |url=https://voxeu.org/article/building-effective-short-time-work-schemes-covid-19-crisis |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=VoxEU.org}}</ref><ref name="IMF-2022">{{Cite web |title=Policy Responses to COVID19 |url=https://www.imf.org/en/Topics/imf-and-covid19/Policy-Responses-to-COVID-19 |access-date=2022-06-05 |website=IMF |language=en}}</ref>

==== Surveys and studies ====
In a paper published in early May 2020, the [[Urban Institute]] estimated that about 25 million people would lose their employer-provided health insurance if the unemployment rate were to rise to 20%. Of these, they speculated, 12&nbsp;million would obtain Medicaid coverage, 6&nbsp;million would find coverage privately, and 7&nbsp;million would become uninsured.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Garrett|first1=Owen|date=May 4, 2020|title=How the COVID-19 Recession Could Affect Health Insurance Coverage|work=urban.org|url=https://www.urban.org/research/publication/how-covid-19-recession-could-affect-health-insurance-coverage|access-date=May 15, 2020}}</ref>

By summer 2020, nearly two-thirds of households where an adult had contracted COVID-19 had also experienced job loss or a reduction in hours. Likewise, "serious financial problems" were reported by nearly two-thirds of those households, whereas such problems were reported by just under half of U.S. households overall, according to a survey by [[NPR]], the [[Robert Wood Johnson Foundation]], and the T.H. Chan School of Public Health at Harvard University that was conducted in July and August.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Peck|first=Emily|date=September 23, 2020|title=Getting COVID-19 Is Putting Americans On The Brink Of Economic Crisis|url=https://www.huffpost.com/entry/covid-19-putting-americans-on-brink-of-economic-crisis_n_5f6a76c2c5b629afbe97e35e|access-date=September 23, 2020|website=HuffPost|language=en}}</ref> 

The impact of COVID-19 on employment varied widely across industries. For example, a study found that transportation workers were 20.6% more likely to be unemployed compared to those in other sectors, with specific sub-sectors like taxi drivers facing particularly severe job losses.<ref>{{Cite journal |last1=Mack |first1=Elizabeth A. |last2=Agrawal |first2=Shubham |last3=Wang |first3=Sicheng |date=December 2021 |title=The impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic on transportation employment: A comparative analysis |journal=Transportation Research Interdisciplinary Perspectives |language=en |volume=12 |article-number=100470 |doi=10.1016/j.trip.2021.100470 |pmc=8450065 |pmid=34568808|bibcode=2021TrRIP..1200470M }}</ref>

===Eviction and foreclosure===
{{Further|COVID-19 eviction moratoriums in the United States}}
The economic impact and mass unemployment caused by the COVID-19 pandemic raised fears of a mass [[Eviction in the United States|eviction crisis]], with an analysis by the [[Aspen Institute]] indicating between 19 and 23 million, or 1 in 5 renters, were at risk for eviction by the end of September 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|date=June 19, 2020|title=20 Million Renters Are at Risk of Eviction; Policymakers Must Act Now to Mitigate Widespread Hardship|url=https://www.aspeninstitute.org/blog-posts/20-million-renters-are-at-risk-of-eviction/|access-date=July 11, 2020|publisher=The Aspen Institute|language=en-US}}</ref> A separate July 2021 [[United States Census Bureau]] survey projects 7&nbsp;million households unable to pay rent and at risk of eviction, with a potential 3&nbsp;million eviction filings in the next two months.<ref>{{cite news |last= |first= |date=2021-07-14 |title=Week 33 Household Pulse Survey: June 23 – July 5 |url=https://www.census.gov/data/tables/2021/demo/hhp/hhp33.html |url-status=live |work=[[United States Census Bureau]] |location= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210806182906/https://www.census.gov/data/tables/2021/demo/hhp/hhp33.html |archive-date=2021-08-06 |access-date=2021-08-08}}</ref>

The federal [[CARES Act]] provided for a 120-day moratorium on evictions for federally backed properties, beginning on March 27 when the act was signed, and lasting til July 24<ref>{{Cite web|title=Protections for renters|url=https://www.consumerfinance.gov/coronavirus/mortgage-and-housing-assistance/renter-protections/|access-date=July 11, 2020|website=Consumer Financial Protection Bureau|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{cite report|url=https://www.hud.gov/sites/dfiles/CPD/documents/HOME-FAQs-COVID-19.pdf|title=HOME Investment Partnerships Program FAQs – COVID-19 & CARES Act|author=[[United States Department of Housing and Urban Development]]|date=May 1, 2020}}</ref> on eviction filings for rental units in properties that participate in federal assistance programs, or have a federally backed mortgage or multifamily mortgage loan.<ref>{{cite report|url=https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/IN/IN11320|title=CARES Act Eviction Moratorium|last1=McCarty|first1=Maggie|last2=Carpenter|first2=David|date=April 7, 2020|publisher=[[Congressional Research Service]]|number=IN11320}}</ref> According to one estimate, this eviction moratorium covered 28% of all rental units in the United States;<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=Goodman|first1=Laurie|last2=Kaul|first2=Karan|last3=Neal|first3=Michael|date=April 2, 2020|title=The CARES Act Eviction Moratorium Covers All Federally Financed Rentals—That's One in Four US Rental Units|url=https://www.urban.org/urban-wire/cares-act-eviction-moratorium-covers-all-federally-financed-rentals-thats-one-four-us-rental-units|access-date=July 10, 2020|journal=Urban Institute|language=en}}</ref> however, there were no enforcement mechanisms provided.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Ernsthausen|first1=Jeff|last2=Simani|first2=Ellis|last3=Elliott|first3=Justin|title=Despite Federal Ban, Landlords Are Still Moving to Evict People During the Pandemic|url=https://www.propublica.org/article/despite-federal-ban-landlords-are-still-moving-to-evict-people-during-the-pandemic?token=Gg58888u2U5db3W3CsuKrD0LD_VQJReQ|access-date=July 10, 2020|website=ProPublica|date=April 16, 2020 |language=en}}</ref> A number of cities and states also passed rulings suspending evictions for varying amounts of time.<ref>{{Cite web|date=March 18, 2020|title=More cities, states are halting evictions because of COVID-19|url=https://www.marketplace.org/2020/03/18/growing-number-of-cities-states-halting-evictions-because-of-coronavirus/|access-date=July 18, 2020|website=Marketplace|language=en-US}}</ref> When these moratoria ended in June and July 2020, evictions increased in many states and jurisdictions.<ref>{{Cite web|title=As COVID-19 Safeguards End, Eviction Wave Begins – Law360|url=https://www.law360.com/articles/1290313/as-covid-19-safeguards-end-eviction-wave-begins|access-date=July 18, 2020|website=law360.com|language=en}}</ref> A new nationwide moratorium was announced by the [[Centers for Disease Control]] began on September 1 which banned evictions for most renters for the rest of 2020.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/09/01/business/eviction-moratorium-order.html |title=U.S. Orders Eviction Moratorium for Most Through Year's End: The C.D.C. action cited the coronavirus risk if tenants are forced into shelters or other crowded quarters. It did not lift their rent obligation |first=Matthew |last=Goldstein |work=The New York Times |date=September 1, 2020}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.foxbusiness.com/money/trumps-new-eviction-moratorium-everything-renters-need-to-know |title=Trump's new eviction moratorium: Everything renters need to know |first=Megan |last=Henney |publisher=Fox Business |date=September 3, 2020 |access-date=September 4, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231417/https://www.foxbusiness.com/money/trumps-new-eviction-moratorium-everything-renters-need-to-know |url-status=live }}</ref>

The CARES Act also provided protection from foreclosure until August 31, 2020, for homeowners with federally backed mortgages.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Learn about mortgage relief options|url=https://www.consumerfinance.gov/coronavirus/mortgage-and-housing-assistance/mortgage-relief/|access-date=July 20, 2020|website=Consumer Financial Protection Bureau|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231212/https://www.consumerfinance.gov/coronavirus/mortgage-and-housing-assistance/mortgage-relief/|url-status=live}}</ref> The CARES Act also allows mortgage holders the right to a mortgage forbearance for up to 180 days, with another 180 days on request.<ref>{{cite report|author=National Consumer Law Center|title=Coronavirus Emergency: What Consumers Need to Know about Mortgage Relief|url=https://www.nclc.org/images/pdf/special_projects/covid-19/IB_Consumer_Mortgage_Relief.pdf|date=April 2020|access-date=July 22, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231024/https://www.nclc.org/images/pdf/special_projects/covid-19/IB_Consumer_Mortgage_Relief.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|title=Learn about mortgage relief options|url=https://www.consumerfinance.gov/coronavirus/mortgage-and-housing-assistance/mortgage-relief/|access-date=July 10, 2020|website=Consumer Financial Protection Bureau|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231153/https://www.consumerfinance.gov/coronavirus/mortgage-and-housing-assistance/mortgage-relief/|url-status=live}}</ref> Several states also passed foreclosure moratoriums as well.<ref>{{Cite web|author=National Consumer Law Center|date=March 20, 2020|title=Covid-19 State Foreclosure Moratoriums and Stays|url=https://www.nclc.org/issues/foreclosures-and-mortgages/covid-19-state-foreclosure-moratoriums-and-stays.html|access-date=July 20, 2020|website=National Consumer Law Center|language=en-US|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231405/https://www.nclc.org/issues/foreclosures-and-mortgages/covid-19-state-foreclosure-moratoriums-and-stays.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

The [[United States Census Bureau]] uses their Household Pulse Survey<ref>{{cite news |last= |first= |date= |title=Household Pulse Survey Data Tables |url=https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/household-pulse-survey/data.html |url-status=live |work=[[United States Census Bureau]] |location= |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210806171510/https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/household-pulse-survey/data.html |archive-date=2021-08-06 |access-date=2021-08-08}}</ref> to gather data on both monthly rent and mortgage payments during the pandemic. For the week of July 16–21, 2020, 8,251,079 homeowners of owner-occupied housing units did not make their mortgage payment on time and 4,473,321 deferred their payments, out of 148,685,473 surveyed.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/demo/tables/hhp/2020/wk12/housing1a_week12.xlsx |title=Week 12 Household Pulse Survey: July 16 – July 21 |date=July 21, 2020 |website=census.gov |publisher=[[United States Census Bureau]] |access-date=August 9, 2020 |url-status=live |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200810051748/https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/demo/tables/hhp/2020/wk12/housing1a_week12.xlsx |archive-date=August 10, 2020 }}</ref> Similarly, 13,339,515 renters did not pay last month's rent, and 1,504,864 deferred their rent, out of 73,065,587 responses.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/demo/tables/hhp/2020/wk12/housing1b_week12.xlsx |title=Week 12 Household Pulse Survey: July 16 – July 21 |date=July 21, 2020 |website=census.gov |publisher=[[United States Census Bureau]] |access-date=August 9, 2020 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200810051740/https://www2.census.gov/programs-surveys/demo/tables/hhp/2020/wk12/housing1b_week12.xlsx |archive-date=August 10, 2020 }}</ref>

[[Homelessness]] had already been rising before the pandemic, with a 2% increase in the number of homeless people between January 2019 and January 2020.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Fessler|first=Pam|date=March 18, 2021|title=HUD: Growth Of Homelessness During 2020 Was 'Devastating,' Even Before The Pandemic|url=https://www.npr.org/2021/03/18/978244891/hud-growth-of-homelessness-during-2020-was-devastating-even-before-the-pandemic|access-date=March 18, 2021|publisher=NPR|language=en}}</ref>

===Home sales===
People who could afford to buy homes enjoyed the lowest mortgage interest rates since at least 1971.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Arnold |first1=Chris |url=https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/07/21/893493664/record-low-mortgage-rates-put-more-cash-in-pockets-but-may-not-boost-economy-muc|title=Record Low Mortgage Rates Put More Cash In Pockets, But May Not Boost Economy Much|website=NPR.org|date=July 21, 2020 |publisher=NPR|access-date=August 15, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231407/https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/07/21/893493664/record-low-mortgage-rates-put-more-cash-in-pockets-but-may-not-boost-economy-muc|url-status=live}}</ref> Home sales rose and showed a trend of people who could work from home moving out of expensive, large cities into smaller, lower-cost cities where they could afford larger homes with more on-site amenities.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Sanders |first1=Sam |url=https://www.npr.org/2020/08/12/901956796/the-good-bad-and-ugly-of-the-pandemic-housing-market-plus-tiktok-under-fire|title=The Good, Bad And Ugly Of The Pandemic Housing Market, Plus TikTok Under Fire: It's Been a Minute with Sam Sanders|website=NPR.org|date=August 14, 2020 |publisher=NPR|access-date=August 15, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231151/https://www.npr.org/2020/08/12/901956796/the-good-bad-and-ugly-of-the-pandemic-housing-market-plus-tiktok-under-fire|url-status=live}}</ref>
States like California and New York saw an exodus as a result of the pandemic.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.cnn.com/2021/02/01/success/new-york-california-exodus-2020-pandemic/index.html|title=Lots of people moved out of New York and California in 2020. Here's where they went|date=February 2021 |publisher=CNN}}</ref>
Housing prices rose amid a building boom as lumber futures reached an all-time high.<ref>{{Cite news |last1=Dezember |first1=Ryan |title=Lumber Prices Notch Records on Building, Remodeling Boom |work=[[The Wall Street Journal]] |date=February 16, 2021 |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/lumber-prices-notch-records-on-building-remodeling-boom-11613471400 |language=en-US |issn=0099-9660  }}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last1=Dezember |first1=Ryan |last2=Monga |first2=Vipal |title=Lumber Prices Are Soaring. Why Are Tree Growers Miserable? |work=The Wall Street Journal |date=February 24, 2021 |url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/lumber-prices-are-soaring-tree-growers-miserable-11614177282 |language=en-US |issn=0099-9660  }}</ref>

=== Food insecurity ===

Food insecurity across the United States [[Hunger in the United States#Coronavirus (COVID-19) pandemic and food insecurity|rose significantly in 2020]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Unemployment rose higher in three months of COVID-19 than it did in two years of the Great Recession|url=https://www.pewresearch.org/fact-tank/2020/06/11/unemployment-rose-higher-in-three-months-of-covid-19-than-it-did-in-two-years-of-the-great-recession/|access-date=April 11, 2021|website=Pew Research Center|date=June 11, 2020 |language=en-US}}</ref> Feeding America predicts that an additional 17 million people could become food insecure because of the pandemic, bringing the total to more than 54&nbsp;million people in the country.{{cn|date=June 2021}}

Before COVID-19, food insecurity was still prevalent, impacting 37&nbsp;million people in the U.S. Since food insecurity and poor nutrition are "associated with chronic diseases that put people at higher risk for the more severe complications of COVID-19, the food access crisis has exacerbated the already glaring disparities in health outcomes for vulnerable people, including low-income people, children, older adults, and [undocumented immigrants living in the United States]."<ref name="aamc.org">{{Cite web|title=54 million people in America face food insecurity during the pandemic. It could have dire consequences for their health|url=https://www.aamc.org/news-insights/54-million-people-america-face-food-insecurity-during-pandemic-it-could-have-dire-consequences-their|access-date=April 11, 2021|website=AAMC|language=en}}</ref> While the factors underlying racial and ethnic disparities are complex and multidimensional, disparities in food security have played a crucial role in the health inequities that have been witnessed during the pandemic. The ability to eat healthy, nutritious food depends on an individual's circumstances and environment.<ref name="aamc.org"/><ref>{{Cite web|title=USDA ERS – Home|url=https://www.ers.usda.gov/|access-date=April 11, 2021|website=ers.usda.gov}}</ref>

The pandemic left millions of people in the U.S. at-risk when it comes to nutrition and overall health status. The pandemic complicated food insecurity among children, older adults, and undocumented immigrants. Feeding America stated that the estimated number of food-insecure kids could jump from 11 million to an estimated 18&nbsp;million. Additionally, many children in the U.S. rely on free meals for sustenance. While food banks and pantries amped up services, the temporary disruption in these services could have devastating long-term consequences. Many seniors live on low and fixed incomes, resulting in decreased access to adequate nutrition and support services. Seniors are also particularly vulnerable to the devastating effects of COVID-19, leading many to avoid unnecessary visits to food pantries and health clinics. Undocumented immigrants are arguably the most vulnerable to food insecurity because they are ineligible for many government assistance programs. Even before the pandemic, 1 in 4 experienced food insecurity.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Bread for the World|url=https://www.bread.org/|access-date=April 11, 2021|website=Bread for the World|language=en}}</ref>

Food pantries encountered record numbers of clients, with many across the country serving 55 percent more people now than before the pandemic, according to Feeding America. The pandemic further stretched existing community resources, and it demonstrated the need for additional funding geared towards addressing the largest contributing factors of health – the social determinants of health.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Home Page|url=https://www.hpoe.org/|access-date=April 11, 2021|website=hpoe.org}}</ref>

== Impact by economic sector ==

=== Financial market impacts ===

{{See also|Financial market impact of the COVID-19 pandemic|2020 stock market crash}}
[[File:March 3, 2020 FOMC Press Conference NZ73153 (49614546592).jpg|thumb|March 3: Federal Reserve chairman [[Jerome Powell]] announces a 50 basis point (0.5% percentage point) interest rate cut in light of "evolving risks to economic activity."]]

On February 27, 2020, the [[Dow Jones Industrial Average]] (DJIA) dropped 1,191 points, the [[List of largest daily changes in the Dow Jones Industrial Average|largest single-day point drop]] in the index's history at the time; some attributed the drop to anxiety about the epidemic.<ref name="USA Today Dow Feb 27">{{cite web|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/02/27/dow-markets-plunge-correction-coronavirus/4889977002/|title=Dow plunges 1,191 points, its biggest one-day point drop, as coronavirus fears escalate|last=Menton|first=Jessica|date=February 27, 2020|website=USA Today|access-date=February 28, 2020|archive-date=July 27, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200727015314/https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/02/27/dow-markets-plunge-correction-coronavirus/4889977002/|url-status=live}}</ref> The same day, the S&P 500 logged a 4.4% decline.<ref name="CNBC SP 500 Feb 27">{{cite web |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/02/27/this-is-the-fastest-stock-market-correction-in-history.html |title=It took stocks only six days to fall into correction, the fastest drop in history |last=Li |first=Yun |date=February 27, 2020 |publisher=[[CNBC]] |access-date=February 28, 2020 |archive-date=October 31, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201031110844/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/02/27/this-is-the-fastest-stock-market-correction-in-history.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The record was beaten five more times during the outbreak on March{{nbsp}}9 (2,013), March 11 (1,465), March 12 (2,353), and finally set the current record for most points lost in a single day by losing 2,997 points on March 16. It once again fell another 1,338 points on March 18. On March 13, the stock market rebounded for the single largest one-day point gain in the market's history by gaining 1,985 points after Trump declared a state of national emergency to free up resources to combat the virus.<ref>{{cite web |last1=Winck |first1=Ben |title=Live updates: Dow rebounds sharply a day after index tanked its most since 1987 on coronavirus worries |url=https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/stock-market-news-today-index-reaction-coronavirus-global-stimulus-measures-2020-3-1028994772 |website=Markets Insider |publisher=Insider |access-date=March 14, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231359/https://markets.businessinsider.com/news/stocks/stock-market-news-today-index-reaction-coronavirus-global-stimulus-measures-2020-3-1028994772 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |title=Dow Jones Industrial Average |url=https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/djia |website=MarketWatch |access-date=March 14, 2020 |archive-date=February 6, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200206132349/https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/djia |url-status=live }}</ref> The six business days it took for the [[S&P 500 Index]] to drop 10% (from February 20 to 27) "marked the quickest 10% decline from an all-time high in the index's history."<ref name="CNBC SP 500 Feb 27" /> From January 21 to March 1, the DJIA dropped more than 3,500 points, equating to roughly a 13% decrease.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/02/28/the-dow-just-lost-12percent-in-one-week-heres-why-and-what-likely-happens-next.html |title=The Dow lost 12% in one week. Here's why and what likely happens next |last=Imbert |first=Fred |website=CNBC—Markets |date=February 28, 2020 |publisher=CNBC |access-date=March 2, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200302072632/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/02/28/the-dow-just-lost-12percent-in-one-week-heres-why-and-what-likely-happens-next.html |archive-date=March 2, 2020 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/djia |title=Dow Jones Industrial Average—Overview |website=MarketWatch |access-date=February 29, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200206132349/https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/djia |archive-date=February 6, 2020 |url-status=live }}</ref>

Stock index [[futures contract|futures]] declined sharply during Trump's March 11 address,<ref>{{cite web|date=March 12, 2020|title=U.S. Index Futures Sink After Trump Announces Europe Travel Ban|url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2020-03-12/u-s-index-futures-fall-after-trump-addresses-nation-k7o2ok5g|access-date=March 22, 2020|website=Bloomberg}}</ref> and the [[Dow Jones Industrial Average|Dow Jones]] declined 10% the following day—the largest daily decline since [[Black Monday (1987)|Black Monday]] in 1987—despite the Federal Reserve also announcing it would inject $1.5&nbsp;trillion into money markets.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/us-policy/2020/03/12/markets-stocks-today-coronavirus/|title=U.S. stock market suffers worst crash since 1987, as Americans wake up to a new normal of life|newspaper=The Washington Post|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=March 13, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200313110620/https://www.washingtonpost.com/us-policy/2020/03/12/markets-stocks-today-coronavirus/|url-status=live}}</ref> By March 18, investors were shunning even assets considered safe havens during economic crises, such as government bonds and gold, moving into cash positions.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/markets-enter-new-phasewhere-cash-is-all-that-matters-11584546863|title=Markets Enter New Phase—Where Cash Is All That Matters|first=Paul J.|last=Davies|date=March 18, 2020|work=The Wall Street Journal|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231158/https://www.wsj.com/articles/markets-enter-new-phasewhere-cash-is-all-that-matters-11584546863|url-status=live}}</ref> By March 20, the Dow Jones was below the level when President Trump was inaugurated on January 20, 2017, having fallen 35% from its February peak.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/03/20/business/coronavirus-trump-stock-market.html |title=Stock market's gain under Trump vanishes |website=The New York Times |date=March 20, 2020 |access-date=May 27, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231159/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/03/20/business/coronavirus-trump-stock-market.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The markets rallied between March 23 and 26, with the Dow having its best three-day gain since 1931. On March 27, the Dow fell 3.5% and the S&P 500 fell 3.2%. The NASDAQ Index also fell. [[Boeing]] fell 10%, while [[Exxon]] and [[Disney]] each fell 6%.<ref>[https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/26/stock-market-futures-open-to-close-news.html Dow tumbles more than 700 points following a massive 3-day bounce] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231217/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/26/stock-market-futures-open-to-close-news.html |date=February 14, 2021 }} by Fred Imbert, CNBC, March 26, 2020</ref>

In February 2020, the American companies [[Apple Inc.]] and [[Microsoft]] began lowering expectations for revenue because of supply chain disruptions in China caused by the virus.<ref name="Forbes MSFT">{{cite web|last=Rash|first=Wayne|title=Microsoft Warning Is A Harbinger Of Coronavirus Impact On Business and the Economy|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/waynerash/2020/02/27/microsoft-warning-is-a-harbinger-of-coronavirus-impact-on-business-and-the-economy/|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200228072030/https://www.forbes.com/sites/waynerash/2020/02/27/microsoft-warning-is-a-harbinger-of-coronavirus-impact-on-business-and-the-economy/#628fd0f07449|archive-date=February 28, 2020|access-date=February 28, 2020|website=[[Forbes]]}}</ref> In a February 27 note to clients, [[Goldman Sachs]] said it expects no earnings growth for U.S. companies in 2020 as a result of the virus, at a time when the consensus forecast of Wall Street expected "earnings to climb 7%."<ref name="CNBC Goldman">{{cite web|last=Fitzgerald|first=Maggie|title=Goldman sees zero earnings growth for US companies this year because of coronavirus|date=February 27, 2020|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/02/27/goldman-sees-zero-earnings-growth-for-us-companies-this-year-because-of-coronavirus.html|access-date=February 28, 2020|publisher=[[CNBC]]}}</ref> On March 20, 2020, as part of an [[U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission|SEC]] filing, [[AT&T]] cancelled all [[Share repurchase|stock buyback]] plans included a plan to repurchase stock worth $4&nbsp;billion during the second quarter. The reasons AT&T gave for the cancellation was to invest the money into its networks and in taking care of its employees during the pandemic.<ref>{{cite web|last=Spangler|first=Todd|date=March 20, 2020|title=AT&T Warns Coronavirus Financial Impact 'Could Be Material', Nixes $4 Billion Stock-Buyback Plan|url=https://variety.com/2020/biz/news/att-coronavirus-material-cancels-stock-buyback-plan-1203540168/|access-date=March 20, 2020|website=Variety|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231154/https://variety.com/2020/biz/news/att-coronavirus-material-cancels-stock-buyback-plan-1203540168/|url-status=live}}</ref>

In response to the economic damage caused by the pandemic, some economists have advocated for financial support from the government for individual Americans and for banks and businesses.<ref name="NYT_Golden">{{cite news|last=Golden|first=Ian|title=Can We Stop a Coronavirus Recession?|work=The New York Times |date=March 12, 2020 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/03/12/opinion/coronavirus-economy.html?action=click&module=Opinion&pgtype=Homepage|access-date=March 12, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231201/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/03/12/opinion/coronavirus-economy.html?action=click&module=Opinion&pgtype=Homepage|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="Mankiw_Blog">{{cite web|last=Mankiw|first=Greg|title=Thoughts on the Pandemic|url=http://gregmankiw.blogspot.com/2020/03/thoughts-on-pandemic.html|access-date=March 13, 2020|website=gregmankiw.blogstpot.com|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231153/http://gregmankiw.blogspot.com/2020/03/thoughts-on-pandemic.html|url-status=live}}</ref> Financial analyst [[Karen Shaw Petrou|Karen Petrou]] objected to government intervention on the grounds that it would alter the role of the [[Federal Reserve]] and enshrine [[moral hazard]] as a defining market principle.<ref>{{cite news|last1=Petrou|first1=Karen|date=March 1, 2020|title=Federal Reserve should not try to fight the coronavirus|work=[[Financial Times]]|url=https://www.ft.com/content/d3bf5890-5ba0-11ea-b0ab-339c2307bcd4|access-date=March 17, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231304/https://www.ft.com/content/d3bf5890-5ba0-11ea-b0ab-339c2307bcd4|url-status=live}}</ref>

Several officials [[2020 Congressional insider trading scandal|faced allegations]] of [[insider trading]], citing sales of their stock portfolios that coincided with private briefings and other statements regarding COVID-19, and almost immediately preceding [[2020 stock market crash|a major stock market crash on February 20]]. [[Senate Intelligence Committee]] chairman [[Richard Burr]] sold up to $1.7&nbsp;million of stocks while making public statements of reassurances of the government's level of COVID-19 preparedness. Georgia senator [[Kelly Loeffler]] sold tens of millions of dollars' worth of stocks after a closed briefing on the COVID-19.<ref name="ProPublicaBurr">{{Cite news|title=Senator Dumped Up to $1.7 Million of Stock After Reassuring Public About Coronavirus Preparedness|publisher=ProPublica|url=https://www.propublica.org/article/senator-dumped-up-to-1-6-million-of-stock-after-reassuring-public-about-coronavirus-preparedness|access-date=April 14, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200411102950/https://www.propublica.org/article/senator-dumped-up-to-1-7-million-of-stock-after-reassuring-public-about-coronavirus-preparedness|archive-date=April 11, 2020}}</ref> Senator Dianne Feinstein, a member of the Senate intelligence committee, sold between $1.5m and $6m in stock of Allogene Therapeutics, a biotechnology company. U.S. Representative Nancy Pelosi's husband purchased $3.3&nbsp;million worth of technology stocks expected to surge during a lockdown.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Strauss|first=Daniel|date=April 13, 2020|title='Unfair advantage': concerns US politicians are using coronavirus briefings to make stock decisions|language=en-GB|work=The Guardian|url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/apr/13/unfair-advantage-concerns-us-lawmakers-are-using-coronavirus-briefings-to-make-stock-decisions|access-date=April 19, 2020|issn=0261-3077|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231203/https://www.theguardian.com/world/2020/apr/13/unfair-advantage-concerns-us-lawmakers-are-using-coronavirus-briefings-to-make-stock-decisions|url-status=live}}</ref> On May 14, after having had a warrant served against him by the FBI, Burr announced that he would step down from his position during the investigation.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Wilkie|first=Christina|date=May 14, 2020|title=Sen. Richard Burr steps down as Senate intel chair during coronavirus stock sale probe|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/05/14/sen-richard-burr-steps-down-as-senate-intel-chair-during-coronavirus-stock-sale-probe.html|access-date=May 14, 2020|publisher=CNBC|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231207/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/05/14/sen-richard-burr-steps-down-as-senate-intel-chair-during-coronavirus-stock-sale-probe.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In 2021, 79% of US firms stated that the COVID-19 pandemic will have a long-term influence on requirements and priorities in investment.<ref>{{Cite web |title=JRC Publications Repository |url=https://publications.jrc.ec.europa.eu/repository/handle/JRC125544 |access-date=2022-02-09 |website=publications.jrc.ec.europa.eu|date=June 23, 2021 }}</ref><ref name="Bank-2022a">{{Cite book |last=Bank |first=European Investment |url=https://www.eib.org/en/publications/investment-report-2021 |title=EIB Investment Report 2021/2022: Recovery as a springboard for change |date=2022-01-12 |publisher=European Investment Bank |isbn=978-92-861-5155-2 |language=EN}}</ref>

The supply of coins in the U.S. banking system was lowered to the point of rationing, due to many stores being closed, many coin-counting machines being unavailable, and fewer coins produced by the U.S. mint to protect employees.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.npr.org/2020/06/21/880958665/the-latest-pandemic-shortage-coins-are-the-new-toilet-paper|title=The Latest Pandemic Shortage: Coins Are The New Toilet Paper|date=June 21, 2020|access-date=July 25, 2020|publisher=NPR|last=Horsely|first=Scott|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231159/https://www.npr.org/2020/06/21/880958665/the-latest-pandemic-shortage-coins-are-the-new-toilet-paper|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2020/07/21/fact-check-america-midst-national-coin-shortage/5439455002/|title=Fact check: Yes, there's a national coin shortage. Here's why|last=Richardson|first=Ian|website=[[USA Today]]|date=July 22, 2020|access-date=July 25, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231306/https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/factcheck/2020/07/21/fact-check-america-midst-national-coin-shortage/5439455002/|url-status=live}}</ref>

However, as the economy recovered from COVID-19, some such as J.P. Morgan Chase strategist Marko Kolanovic expected the U.S. stock market surge that happened since the trough to continue into 2021.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Cox|first=Jeff|date=December 9, 2020|title=JP Morgan strategist who called March market comeback sees a big year ahead for stocks|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/12/09/jp-morgan-strategist-who-called-march-market-comeback-sees-a-big-year-ahead-for-stocks.html|access-date=December 10, 2020|publisher=CNBC|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231410/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/12/09/jp-morgan-strategist-who-called-march-market-comeback-sees-a-big-year-ahead-for-stocks.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== Global Supply Chain ===
Due to lockdowns, the global trade volume were contracted by 16% in the first two quarters of 2020<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Attinasi |first=Maria Grazia |last2=Boeckelmann |first2=Lukas |last3=Hespert |first3=Laura |last4=Linzenich |first4=Jan |last5=Meunier |first5=Baptiste |date=2024-02-08 |title=Global trade in the post-pandemic environment |url=https://www.ecb.europa.eu/press/economic-bulletin/focus/2024/html/ecb.ebbox202401_01~d1c3b1b0a5.en.html |journal=ECB economics bulletin |language=en}}</ref>. The effects on the [https://www.slimstock.com/blog/global-supply-chain/ global supply chain] were devastating, as the production of raw materials, intermediate goods, and finished goods suddenly became scarce due to labor shortages. Companies that rely on the technique of [https://www.investopedia.com/terms/j/jit.asp Just-In-Time (JIT)] experienced significant inventory shortages. While JIT is an effective method that reduces excess inventories and relieves costs for companies, it increases firms' vulnerability to supply shocks.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Xu |first=Zhitao |last2=Elomri |first2=Adel |last3=Kerbache |first3=Laoucine |last4=El Omri |first4=Abdelfatteh |date=2020-09-01 |title=Impacts of COVID-19 on Global Supply Chains: Facts and Perspectives |url=https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/9174793/ |journal=IEEE Engineering Management Review |volume=48 |issue=3 |pages=153–166 |doi=10.1109/EMR.2020.3018420 |issn=0360-8581 |pmc=8768947}}</ref>

Supply chain disruptions should not be overlooked, 80% of firms view supply chain resilience as a top priority. <ref>{{Cite journal |last=Ambulkar |first=Saurabh |last2=Blackhurst |first2=Jennifer |last3=Grawe |first3=Scott |date=2015-01-01 |title=Firm's resilience to supply chain disruptions: Scale development and empirical examination |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0272696314000874 |journal=Journal of Operations Management |volume=33-34 |pages=111–122 |doi=10.1016/j.jom.2014.11.002 |issn=0272-6963|url-access=subscription }}</ref>

=== Energy sector ===
{{See also|2020 oil industry financial crisis|2020 Russia–Saudi Arabia oil price war}}
The U.S. oil, gas and chemicals industry lost 107,000 jobs between March and August 2020, according to Deloitte. When demand for jet fuel, diesel and gasoline suddenly dropped, oil was in oversupply.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Egan|first=Matt|date=October 8, 2020|title=The pandemic made 107,000 oil and gas jobs disappear. Most aren't coming back anytime soon|url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/10/08/business/oil-gas-jobs/index.html|access-date=October 8, 2020|publisher=CNN|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231156/https://www.cnn.com/2020/10/08/business/oil-gas-jobs/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== Health care sector ===

==== Production of emergency supplies ====

{{See also|Shortages related to the COVID-19 pandemic}}
[[File:Rhode Island National Guard.jpg|thumb|A [[Rhode Island National Guard]]sman sews [[cloth face mask]]s on April{{nbsp}}6, 2020.]]

In response to shortages, some alcoholic beverage facilities started manufacturing and distributing alcohol-based [[hand sanitizer]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.npr.org/2020/03/21/817976872/white-house-to-give-an-update-on-coronavirus-tests-response|title=Pence Tests Negative For COVID-19; 'Hundreds Of Millions' Of Face Masks in Production|website=NPR|date=March 21, 2020|access-date=March 24, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231156/https://www.npr.org/2020/03/21/817976872/white-house-to-give-an-update-on-coronavirus-tests-response|url-status=live|last1=Sprunt|first1=Barbara}}</ref> [[General Motors]] opened its manufacturing, logistics, and purchasing infrastructure for use by Ventec, a Washington State manufacturer of medical ventilators.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.thedrive.com/news/32691/general-motors-becomes-first-us-automaker-to-aid-in-ventilator-production-ramp-up|title=General Motors Becomes First US Automaker Committed to Ventilator Production Ramp-Up|first=James|last=Gilboy|website=The Drive|date=March 22, 2020 |access-date=March 24, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231154/https://www.thedrive.com/news/32691/general-motors-becomes-first-us-automaker-to-aid-in-ventilator-production-ramp-up|url-status=live}}</ref> As medical mask manufacturers hired hundreds of new workers and increased output,<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/23/metro/honeywell-will-start-making-n95-face-masks-its-smithfield-ri-plant-adding-500-jobs/|title=Honeywell will start making N95 face masks at its Smithfield, R.I., plant, adding 500 jobs|website=The Boston Globe|access-date=March 24, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231219/https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/23/metro/honeywell-will-start-making-n95-face-masks-its-smithfield-ri-plant-adding-500-jobs/|url-status=live}}</ref> in response to urgent requests from hospital workers, volunteers with home sewing machines started producing thousands of non-medical masks which can be sterilized and re-used. Fabric was bought privately or donated by [[Joann Fabrics]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/22/metro/mask-making-projects-are-popping-all-over-crafters-mobilize-against-coronavirus/|title=Mask-making projects are popping all over as crafters mobilize against coronavirus|website=The Boston Globe|access-date=March 24, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231431/https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/22/metro/mask-making-projects-are-popping-all-over-crafters-mobilize-against-coronavirus/|url-status=live}}</ref> The CDC recommended the use of homemade masks (preferably in combination with a full-face splash shield) only as a "last resort" when no other respiratory protective technologies were available, including reused professional masks.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/hcp/ppe-strategy/face-masks.html|title=Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19)|date=February 11, 2020|website=Centers for Disease Control and Prevention|access-date=March 24, 2020|archive-date=March 23, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200323173927/https://www.cdc.gov/coronavirus/2019-ncov/hcp/ppe-strategy/face-masks.html|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Bauer Hockey]] began manufacturing face shields for medical applications on March 26.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/25/sports/hockey-company-bauer-joins-coronavirus-fight-by-producing-face-shields/|title=New Hampshire-based hockey company Bauer joins coronavirus fight by producing face shields|website=The Boston Globe|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231157/https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/25/sports/hockey-company-bauer-joins-coronavirus-fight-by-producing-face-shields/|url-status=live}}</ref>

Some U.S. officials and commentators criticized the outsourcing of critical materials—like the production of essential medical supplies—to China.<ref>{{cite news |title=Break with China? Top Trump aide eyes an opening with coronavirus |url=https://www.politico.com/news/2020/02/26/trump-china-trade-coronavirus-117531 |work=Politico |date=February 26, 2020 |access-date=May 27, 2020 |archive-date=April 1, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200401190843/https://www.politico.com/news/2020/02/26/trump-china-trade-coronavirus-117531 |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite news |title=Tucker Carlson: Spread of coronavirus is revealing a 'terrifying situation' |url=https://www.foxnews.com/media/tucker-carlson-coronavirus-pandemic-terrifying-situation-china-drugs |publisher=Fox News |date=March 11, 2020}}</ref>

===== International aid =====

Chinese billionaire [[Jack Ma]] donated COVID-19 test kits and face masks to the United States.<ref>{{cite news |title=Chinese billionaire Jack Ma is shipping coronavirus test kits and masks to the U.S. |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/jack-ma-coronavirus-test-kits-chinese-billionaire-alibaba-shipping-masks-supplies-to-united-states-donating/ |publisher=CBS News |date=March 16, 2020}}</ref> The Russian government sent a cargo plane with ventilators and face masks.<ref>{{cite news |title=Russia to the rescue? Trump thanks Putin, but diplomats wary on aid deliveries |url=https://www.timesofisrael.com/russia-to-the-rescue-us-moscow-spar-over-aid-deliveries/ |work=The Times of Israel |date=April 4, 2020}}</ref>

==== Surge in medical personnel ====

Several states and non-profit groups started recruiting retired medical personnel to increase staffing in hospitals and at temporary facilities.<ref>{{cite magazine|url=https://time.com/5810120/retired-health-care-workers-coronavirus/|title=Who Are the Retired Health Care Workers the U.S. is Calling On To Help With the Coronavirus Response?|magazine=Time}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2020/03/31/nation/preparing-coronavirus-surge-massachusetts-is-recruiting-retired-doctors-nurses-respiratory-therapists/|title=Preparing for a surge in cases, Mass. recruits retired doctors, nurses, respiratory therapists |website=The Boston Globe}}</ref> Some jurisdictions granted emergency medical licenses to inactive doctors, and incoming resident doctors and interns, and expanded the tasks that nurses were allowed to do.<ref name="ma-mar27">{{cite web|url=https://www.wbur.org/news/2020/03/27/baker-out-of-state-quarantine|title=Baker Asks Visitors To Self-Isolate For Two Weeks—Or Not Come at All|website=wbur.org|date=March 27, 2020 |access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=May 1, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200501111019/https://www.wbur.org/news/2020/03/27/baker-out-of-state-quarantine|url-status=live}}</ref>

==== Telemedicine ====

The COVID-19 pandemic led to a sharp increase in the use of [[telemedicine|telemedical services]] in the United States, specifically for COVID-19 screening and triage.<ref name="Telemedicine demand">{{cite web |first1=Leah |last1=Rosenbaum |title=The Coronavirus Has Created A Surge Of Telemedicine Demand. GoodRx Now Lets Consumers Compare Services |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/leahrosenbaum/2020/03/26/the-coronavirus-has-created-a-surge-of-telemedicine-demand-goodrx-now-lets-consumers-compare-services/ |work=Forbes |access-date=March 29, 2020 |date=March 26, 2020}}</ref><ref name="Telemedicine surge + Ro's service">{{cite web |first1=Lauren |last1=Clason |title=Telehealth visits surge along with coronavirus cases |url=https://www.rollcall.com/2020/03/24/telehealth-visits-balloon-along-with-coronavirus-cases/ |website=Roll Call |access-date=March 29, 2020 |date=March 24, 2020}}</ref> {{As of|2020|3|29|df=US}}, three companies offered free telemedical screenings for COVID-19 in the United States: K Health (routed through an AI [[chatbot]]), [[Ro (company)|Ro]] (routed through an AI chatbot), and GoodRx (offered through its HeyDoctor platform).<ref name="Free COVID-19 providers">{{cite web |first1=Leah |last1=Rosenbaum |title=Worried That You Have Coronavirus? Here's How To See A Doctor For Free in Your Home |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/leahrosenbaum/2020/03/18/worried-that-you-have-coronavirus-heres-how-to-see-a-doctor-for-free-in-your-home/ |work=Forbes |access-date=March 29, 2020 |date=March 18, 2020}}</ref><ref name="Telemedicine surge + Ro's service" /><ref name="Ro Chatbot">{{cite web |first1=Leah |last1=Rosenbaum |title=Worried About Coronavirus? Now You Can Text Message A Chatbot With Questions |url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/leahrosenbaum/2020/03/12/worried-about-coronavirus-now-you-can-text-message-a-chatbot-with-questions/ |website=Forbes |access-date=March 29, 2020 |date=March 12, 2020}}</ref>

=== Meat industry ===

{{Main|Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the meat industry in the United States}}

[[File:Perdue Chicken Delivery (49875267726).jpg|thumb|May 8, 2020: Distribution of frozen chicken to those in need in [[Baltimore]], Maryland]]

[[Meatpacking plant]]s experienced sporadic shutdowns as many workers contracted COVID-19, resulting in spot meat shortages around the country.{{cn|date=July 2021}}

According to the [[Centers for Disease Control and Prevention]] issued a report stating that by April 27, out of 130,578 workers nationwide, at least 4,913 meat and poultry plant workers had COVID-19. Cases were reported in 115 plants located in 19 states, and at least 20 people had died.<ref name="cdcmmap">[https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/69/wr/mm6918e3.htm?s_cid=mm6918e3_x COVID-19 Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report (MMWR) Among Workers in Meat and Poultry Processing Facilities ― 19 States, April 2020] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231202/https://www.cdc.gov/mmwr/volumes/69/wr/mm6918e3.htm?s_cid=mm6918e3_x |date=February 14, 2021 }} (Early Release / May 1, 2020 / 69) out of approximately 130,000 workers at 115 facilities in 19 states.</ref><ref name="usnmpw">{{cite news | last =Galvin | first =Gaby | title =CDC: Nearly 5,000 Meat Processing Workers Infected by COVID-19 | newspaper =[[U.S. News & World Report]] | date =May 1, 2020 | url =https://www.usnews.com/news/healthiest-communities/articles/2020-05-01/cdc-nearly-5-000-meat-plant-workers-infected-by-coronavirus | access-date =May 2, 2020 | archive-date =May 18, 2020 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200518114054/https://www.usnews.com/news/healthiest-communities/articles/2020-05-01/cdc-nearly-5-000-meat-plant-workers-infected-by-coronavirus | url-status =live }}</ref><ref name="bcold">{{cite news |last1=Waldman |first1=Peter |title=Cold, Crowded, Deadly: How U.S. Meat Plants Became a Virus Breeding Ground |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2020-05-07/coronavirus-closes-meat-plants-threatens-food-supply |publisher=Bloomberg |date=May 7, 2020 |access-date=May 27, 2020 }}</ref> At least 2 [[USDA]] meat inspectors died of COVID-19,<ref name="USAT2">{{cite news | last1 =Bagenstose | first1 =Kyle | last2 =Chadde | first2 =Sky | last3 =Wynn | first3 =Matt | title =USDA inspector dies as coronavirus spreads in meat packing plants. | newspaper =[[USA Today]] | url =https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/2020/04/23/usda-inspector-dies-coronavirus-spreads-meat-packing-plants/3014597001/ | access-date =April 23, 2020 | archive-date =February 14, 2021 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231201/https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/2020/04/23/usda-inspector-dies-coronavirus-spreads-meat-packing-plants/3014597001/ | url-status =live }}</ref>
137 tested positive, and 704 others refused to work due to lack of protective equipment in a high-risk environment.<ref>{{cite news | last =Fernandez | first =Bob | title =A U.S meat inspector asks "Is this the day I contract COVID-19?" Meat inspectors lack basic protective equipment and have to scrounge from the plants they cover | newspaper =[[The Philadelphia Inquirer]] | date =April 24, 2020 | url =https://www.inquirer.com/news/meat-plants-closed-coronavirus-tyson-jbs-cargill-inspectors-shortage-safety-20200424.html | access-date =April 24, 2020 | archive-date =May 7, 2020 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200507072321/https://www.inquirer.com/news/meat-plants-closed-coronavirus-tyson-jbs-cargill-inspectors-shortage-safety-20200424.html | url-status =live }}</ref>

As of April 22, 2020, about 25% of the pork processing capacity of the nation had been cut.<ref name="waterloo">{{cite web|url=https://www.desmoinesregister.com/story/money/agriculture/2020/04/22/coronavirus-in-iowa-tyson-closes-waterloo-pork-processing-meat-packing-plant-covid-19/3002721001/|title=Tyson says it will close its Waterloo pork processing plant indefinitely due to COVID-19|first=Donnelle|last=Eller|website=Des Moines Register}}</ref> Beginning in late March 2020, weekly beef production was down 19% year-over-year.<ref name="meatpacking,Covid19">{{cite web|url=https://www.usatoday.com/in-depth/news/investigations/2020/04/22/meat-packing-plants-covid-may-force-choice-worker-health-food/2995232001/|title=Coronavirus at meatpacking plants worse than first thought, USA Today investigation finds|website=USA Today|date=April 22, 2020 |access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=May 19, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200519021639/https://www.usatoday.com/in-depth/news/investigations/2020/04/22/meat-packing-plants-covid-may-force-choice-worker-health-food/2995232001/|url-status=live}}</ref>
As the fastest-growing mass-market meat animal in the United States, chickens were the most vulnerable to farms running out of capacity to hold an excess population. At least two million chickens were euthanized on farms in Delaware and Maryland rather than slaughtered for meat, due to lack of capacity to process them for human consumption.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/two-million-delmarva-chickens-euthanized-as-virus-hobbles-processing/2020/04/24/82fc93a4-865d-11ea-878a-86477a724bdb_story.html|title=Two million Delmarva chickens euthanized as virus hobbles processing|first1=Michael E.|last1=Ruane|newspaper=The Washington Post|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231225/https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/two-million-delmarva-chickens-euthanized-as-virus-hobbles-processing/2020/04/24/82fc93a4-865d-11ea-878a-86477a724bdb_story.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

By May 5, around 18% of [[Wendy's]] restaurants (concentrated in certain geographic areas of the country) were unable to serve beef sandwiches, but still had chicken available.<ref name="wendys_CNN">{{cite web |last=Valinsky |first=Jordan |url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/05/05/business/wendys-beef-shortage/index.html |title=Wendy's restaurants have a beef shortage |date=May 5, 2020 |publisher=CNN |access-date=May 7, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231200/https://www.cnn.com/2020/05/05/business/wendys-beef-shortage/index.html |url-status=live }}</ref> Wendy's uses fresh beef; in contrast, [[McDonald's]], which uses frozen beef, was unaffected.<ref name="wendys_CNN" /> Supermarket and big box chains [[Kroger]], [[Wegmans]], [[Costco]], [[Whole Foods Market|Whole Foods]], [[ShopRite (United States)|ShopRite]], [[Sam's Club]], [[Stop and Shop]], [[Price Chopper Supermarkets]], and others started limiting the amount of meat purchased per customer.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bostonherald.com/2020/05/04/meat-sales-restricted-at-local-major-grocers-as-supply-chain-volatile-amid-crisis/|title=Meat sales restricted at some grocers as supply chain increasingly volatile amid coronavirus crisis|date=May 4, 2020|access-date=May 21, 2020|work=[[Boston Herald]]|first1=Andrew|last1=Martinez|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231210/https://www.bostonherald.com/2020/05/04/meat-sales-restricted-at-local-major-grocers-as-supply-chain-volatile-amid-crisis/|url-status=live}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.nj.com/coronavirus/2020/05/costco-shoprite-wegmans-whole-foods-others-limit-meat-purchases-to-avoid-food-supply-chain-problems.html|title=Costco, ShopRite, Wegmans, Whole Foods, others limit meat purchases to avoid food supply chain problems|first=Anthony G.|last=Attrino|date=May 5, 2020|website=The Star-Ledger|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231206/https://www.nj.com/coronavirus/2020/05/costco-shoprite-wegmans-whole-foods-others-limit-meat-purchases-to-avoid-food-supply-chain-problems.html|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="CoralMurphy">{{cite news | last =Murphy | first =Coral | title =Costco to temporarily limit meat purchases to 3 items per person | newspaper =USA Today | date =May 4, 2020 | url =https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/05/04/costco-limits-meat-purchases-3-items-per-person/3078198001/ | access-date =May 5, 2020 | archive-date =May 17, 2020 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200517062716/https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/05/04/costco-limits-meat-purchases-3-items-per-person/3078198001/ | url-status =live }}</ref><ref>{{cite news | last =Watertown Daily Times | title =Price Chopper, Tops announce limits on meat | newspaper =[[The Post-Star]] | location =[[Glens Falls, New York]] | date =May 4, 2020 | url =https://poststar.com/news/local/price-chopper-tops-announce-limits-on-meat/article_546f9b98-0f06-5244-a980-e98c1ec59a87.html | access-date =May 5, 2020 | archive-date =May 18, 2020 | archive-url =https://web.archive.org/web/20200518101506/https://poststar.com/news/local/price-chopper-tops-announce-limits-on-meat/article_546f9b98-0f06-5244-a980-e98c1ec59a87.html | url-status =live }}</ref> Hundreds of millions of pounds of beef and pork remain in cold storage, originally intended for restaurants; the USDA started allowing this meat to be sold at grocery stores, though it must be cut down by store workers into consumer-sized packages.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.pennlive.com/coronavirus/2020/04/coronavirus-expected-to-reduce-meat-selection-raise-prices.html|title=Coronavirus expected to reduce meat selection, raise prices; Tyson Foods says 'food supply chain is vulnerable'|agency=Associated Press|date=April 27, 2020|website=pennlive|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231208/https://www.pennlive.com/coronavirus/2020/04/coronavirus-expected-to-reduce-meat-selection-raise-prices.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

=== Restaurant industry ===
{{Main|Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the restaurant industry in the United States}}

[[File:Covid-19 sign, restaurant, Glynn Co, GA, US (cropped).png|thumb|May 11, 2020: sign for curbside restaurant service only in [[Brunswick, Georgia]]]]

The U.S. restaurant industry was projected to have $899&nbsp;billion in sales for 2020 by the [[National Restaurant Association]], the main trade association for the industry in the United States.<ref name="Brownfield-2020">{{cite news |last1=Brownfield |first1=Andy |title=Cincinnati restaurants ask feds for coronavirus bailout |url=https://www.bizjournals.com/cincinnati/news/2020/03/20/cincinnati-restaurants-ask-feds-for-coronavirus.html |work=Cincinnati Business Courier |date=March 20, 2020 |access-date=May 27, 2020 |archive-date=March 26, 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200326024435/https://www.bizjournals.com/cincinnati/news/2020/03/20/cincinnati-restaurants-ask-feds-for-coronavirus.html |url-status=live }}</ref> The industry as a whole as of February 2020 employed more than 15 million people, representing 10 percent of the workforce directly.<ref name="Brownfield-2020" /> It indirectly employed close to another ten percent when dependent businesses such as food producers, trucking, and delivery services were factored in.<ref name="Brownfield-2020" />

On March 15, Ohio Governor [[Mike DeWine]] and Ohio Health Department director [[Amy Acton]] ordered the closure of all bars and restaurants, saying the government "encouraged restaurants to offer carryout or delivery service, but they would not be allowed to have people congregating in the businesses."<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.dispatch.com/news/20200315/coronavirus-governor-orders-ohio-bars-restaurants-to-shut-down|title=Coronavirus: Governor orders Ohio bars, restaurants to shut down|date=March 15, 2020|publisher=The Columbus Dispatch|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200316153943/https://www.dispatch.com/news/20200315/coronavirus-governor-orders-ohio-bars-restaurants-to-shut-down|archive-date=March 16, 2020|access-date=March 15, 2020}}</ref> The next day, Illinois, New York, New Jersey, and Maryland followed suit.<ref name="Conradis-2020">{{cite web|url=https://thehill.com/policy/healthcare/487684-ohio-closing-all-bars-restaurants-in-response-to-coronavirus|title=Illinois, Ohio closing all bars, restaurants in response to coronavirus|last=Conradis|first=Brandon|date=March 15, 2020|website=The Hill|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231206/https://thehill.com/policy/healthcare/487684-illinois-ohio-closing-all-bars-restaurants-in-response-to-coronavirus|url-status=live}}</ref>

Groups of restaurateurs in New York City and Cincinnati called on governments to provide help to the nation's small and independent restaurants.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://thehill.com/changing-america/respect/accessibility/488670-how-nycs-restaurant-industry-is-surviving-amid|title=How NYC's restaurant industry is surviving amid coronavirus closures|last=Somvichian-Clausen|first=Austa|date=March 20, 2020|website=The Hill|access-date=March 22, 2020|archive-date=May 31, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200531042612/https://thehill.com/changing-america/respect/accessibility/488670-how-nycs-restaurant-industry-is-surviving-amid|url-status=live}}</ref><ref name="Brownfield-2020" /> On March 19 the New York group called for state governments to issue orders for rent abatements, suspension of sales and payroll taxes, and a full shutdown so business interruption insurance coverage would be triggered.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://ny.eater.com/2020/3/19/21187117/momofuku-major-food-group-starr-restaurants-relief-coronavirus-nyc|title=NYC's Top Restaurant Groups Band Together to Petition for Government Relief|last=Adams|first=Erika|date=March 19, 2020|website=Eater NY|access-date=March 22, 2020|archive-date=April 3, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200403211510/https://ny.eater.com/2020/3/19/21187117/momofuku-major-food-group-starr-restaurants-relief-coronavirus-nyc|url-status=live}}</ref> On March 20 the Cincinnati group called on the federal government to provide a $225&nbsp;billion bailout to the restaurant industry.<ref name="Brownfield-2020" />

Several restaurant chains altered their operating procedures to prevent the spread of the virus, including removing seating, restricting the use of condiments, and switching to [[mobile payment]] systems. Many restaurants opted to close their dining rooms and instead switch to solely take-out food service to comply with [[physical distancing]] recommendations.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/food/2020/03/15/starbucks-coronavirus-changes-mobile-orders-drive-thru-closing-locations/5055192002/|title=Starbucks closing some locations and moving to a 'to go' model at other stores due to coronavirus|author-last1=Tyko|author-first1= Kelly|date=March 15, 2020|work=USA Today|access-date=March 16, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231433/https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/food/2020/03/15/starbucks-coronavirus-changes-mobile-orders-drive-thru-closing-locations/5055192002/|url-status=live}}</ref>

According to the National Restaurant Association, 60% of restaurant owners did not think the relief programs would be enough to keep employees on payroll. Restaurants reported $30&nbsp;billion in losses in March.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/04/20/coronavirus-restaurants-describe-huge-shortfalls-with-government-aid.html |title=Restaurants describe huge shortfalls within government's coronavirus relief programs |date=April 20, 2020 |publisher=CNBC |access-date=May 27, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231409/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/04/20/coronavirus-restaurants-describe-huge-shortfalls-with-government-aid.html |url-status=live }}</ref>

=== Office workers ===
By 2022, even though it was possible to bring employees back full-time, the necessity of working from home during the pandemic proved to many employers that it was feasible, and many office workers preferred to continue working from home several days a week. Most chose to extend weekends and work from home Monday and Friday. This leaves Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday as high-traffic days in downtown office districts, and negatively impacting patronage at businesses that serve office workers, such as lunch restaurants.<ref name="lunch">{{cite news |url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2022/08/14/business/many-office-workers-are-back-their-favorite-lunch-spots-are-struggling-with-hybrid-work/ |title=Many office workers are back, but their favorite lunch spots are struggling with hybrid work |author-first1=Anissa |author-last1=Gardizy |date=August 14, 2022 |newspaper=[[The Boston Globe]]}}</ref> More businesses began providing free food at offices in order to entice workers to return in person.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2022/03/17/business/when-theres-food-more-people-show-up-return-to-office-plans-spark-catering-boom/ |title=When there's food, 'more people show up': Return-to-office plans spark catering boom |author-first1=Anissa |author-last1=Gardizy |date=March 17, 2022 |newspaper=[[The Boston Globe]]}}</ref> Some companies reconfigured their office spaces to avoid paying for unused desk space. This included leasing out empty space to other companies, and allocating more space to [[hot desk]]s.<ref>{{cite news |url=https://www.bostonglobe.com/2022/06/19/business/some-companies-theres-no-escaping-office-so-theyre-changing-it/ |title=For some companies, there's no escaping the office. So they're changing it. |author-first1=Anissa |author-last1=Gardizy |author-first2=Catherine |author-last2=Carlock |date=June 19, 2022 |newspaper=[[The Boston Globe]]}}</ref>

=== Retail ===

[[File:Best Buy Line I (49674924422).jpg|thumb|March 18: [[Best Buy]] lets only a limited number of people into their Union Square store in New York City.]]{{See also|Retail apocalypse}}
A number of retailers, particularly grocery stores, reduced their opening hours to allow additional time to restock and deep-clean their stores.<ref name=tyko>{{cite web |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/03/14/coronavirus-store-closings-hour-changes/5051282002/ |title=Stores cutting hours or closing because of coronavirus: Walmart, Apple, Nike, Albertsons, Trader Joe's, more |author-last1=Tyko|author-first1= Kelly |work=USA Today |date=March 14, 2020 |access-date=March 16, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231209/https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/03/14/coronavirus-store-closings-hour-changes/5051282002/ |url-status=live }}</ref> Major stores such as [[Walmart]], [[Apple Inc.|Apple]], [[Nike, Inc.|Nike]], [[Albertsons]], and [[Trader Joe's]] also shortened their hours.<ref name=tyko/><ref>[https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20200318005807/en/ Latest Walmart Store Changes to Support Associates and Customers] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231213/https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20200318005807/en/ |date=February 14, 2021 }} Business Wire</ref> Some grocery store chains, including [[Meijer]],<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.mlive.com/news/saginaw-bay-city/2020/04/meijer-to-manage-number-of-customers-in-stores-temporarily-drop-weekly-ad-to-curb-coronavirus.html|title=Meijer to manage number of customers in stores, temporarily drop weekly ad to curb coronavirus|date=April 4, 2020|website=MLive.com|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231200/https://www.mlive.com/news/saginaw-bay-city/2020/04/meijer-to-manage-number-of-customers-in-stores-temporarily-drop-weekly-ad-to-curb-coronavirus.html|url-status=live}}</ref> [[Stop & Shop]] and [[Dollar General]], devoted a portion of their operating hours to serve only senior citizens.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/03/16/coronavirus-seniors-dollar-general-dedicates-hour-senior-shoppers/5065594002/ |title=Dollar General cuts store hours, dedicates hour to senior shoppers during coronavirus pandemic |author-last1=Tyko|author-first1= Kelly |work=USA Today |date=March 16, 2020 |access-date=March 17, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231418/https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2020/03/16/coronavirus-seniors-dollar-general-dedicates-hour-senior-shoppers/5065594002/ |url-status=live }}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.boston.com/food/food/2020/03/16/stop-shop-gives-customers-over-60-their-own-shopping-hours |title=Stop & Shop gives customers over 60 their own shopping hours
