|author-last1=Pickell|author-first1= Jack |work=The Boston Globe |date=March 16, 2020 |access-date=March 17, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231211/https://www.boston.com/food/food/2020/03/16/stop-shop-gives-customers-over-60-their-own-shopping-hours |url-status=live }}</ref> Many grocery stores and pharmacies began installing plexiglass sneeze guards at register areas to protect cashiers and pharmacists, and adding markers six feet apart at checkout lines to encourage customers to maintain physical distance.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/coronavirus-leads-grocers-install-plexiglass-014739290.html|title=Coronavirus leads Walmart, other grocers to install plexiglass to protect employees, customers|date=March 25, 2020 |publisher=Yahoo! Finance|access-date=March 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231207/https://finance.yahoo.com/news/coronavirus-leads-grocers-install-plexiglass-014739290.html|url-status=live}}</ref>
To prevent hoarding, many supermarkets and retailers placed limits on certain products such as toilet paper, hand sanitizer, over-the-counter medication, and cleaning supplies.<ref name="hoarding">{{cite web |url=https://www.politico.com/news/2020/03/15/trump-no-hoarding-coronavirus-grocery-stores-130771 |title=Trump urges no hoarding as coronavirus panic strains grocery stores |author-last1=McCrimmon|author-first1= Ryan |work=Politico |date=March 15, 2020 |access-date=March 16, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231225/https://www.politico.com/news/2020/03/15/trump-no-hoarding-coronavirus-grocery-stores-130771 |url-status=live }}</ref> However, the [[Food Marketing Institute]] announced that its supply chain was not strained and all products would be available in the future.<ref name="hoarding" /> Major retail chains started hiring tens of thousands of employees to keep up with demand, including Walmart (150,000), [[CVS Pharmacy]] (50,000), Dollar General (50,000), and [[7-Eleven]] (20,000).<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.forbes.com/sites/alexandrasternlicht/2020/03/20/whos-hiring-in-the-coronavirus-economy-2736-increase-for-warehouse-handler-7-eleven-adds-20000-postings-more/|title=Updated: Instacart To Hire 300,000 New Shoppers, CVS And Dollar General Add 50,000 Jobs Each—Who's Hiring In Coronavirus Economy|first=Alexandra|last=Sternlicht|website=Forbes|access-date=March 24, 2020}}</ref>
[[Sheetz]] convenience stores began offering free meals to children in need at select stores in Maryland, North Carolina, Ohio, Pennsylvania and West Virginia.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.pennlive.com/coronavirus/2020/04/sheetz-offering-free-kids-meals-to-help-during-coronavirus-pandemic.html|title=Sheetz offering free kids meals to help during coronavirus pandemic|date=April 1, 2020|website=[[The Patriot-News]]|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231201/https://www.pennlive.com/coronavirus/2020/04/sheetz-offering-free-kids-meals-to-help-during-coronavirus-pandemic.html|url-status=live}}</ref> A daily senior shopping hour, checkout line distancing markers, hand washing and sanitizer for employees, disinfecting wipes for customers to use on carts, and a ban on reusable bags became mandatory in Massachusetts on March 25.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.mass.gov/doc/grocery-and-pharmacy-store-order/download|title=Order of the Commissioner of Public Health, March 25 2020|access-date=March 27, 2020|archive-date=November 25, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201125032114/https://www.mass.gov/doc/grocery-and-pharmacy-store-order/download|url-status=live}}</ref> Many stores began limiting the number of people inside at a time, to increase the typical distance between customers, resulting in outdoor lines with people spaced six feet apart.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.wdrb.com/news/target-lowes-home-depot-to-limit-the-number-of-customers-inside-to-combat-spread-of/article_ac5e4948-761a-11ea-b0ce-47befba7acaa.html|title=Target, Lowe's, Home Depot to limit the number of customers inside to combat spread of COVID-19|date=April 4, 2020 |publisher=WDRB|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231205/https://www.wdrb.com/news/target-lowes-home-depot-to-limit-the-number-of-customers-inside-to-combat-spread-of/article_ac5e4948-761a-11ea-b0ce-47befba7acaa.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

Retail sales fell 8.3% in March and 16.4% in April, according to the Commerce Department.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Advance Monthly Sales for Retail and Food Services, April 2020. Release Number: CB20-69|url=https://www.census.gov/retail/marts/www/marts_current.pdf|date=May 15, 2020|website=Census.gov|access-date=May 15, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231048/https://www.census.gov/retail/marts/www/marts_current.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> The hardest-hit sectors included home furnishings (down by two-thirds over these two months) and clothing (down 89% over these two months).<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Casselman|first1=Ben|last2=Maheshwari|first2=Sapna|date=May 15, 2020|title=When Shoppers Venture Out, What Will Be Left?|language=en-US|work=The New York Times|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/05/15/business/economy/retail-sales.html|access-date=May 15, 2020|issn=0362-4331|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231211/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/05/15/business/economy/coronavirus-retail-reopening.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

[[Milk delivery]] services experienced demand which significantly exceeded pre-existing capacity.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.npr.org/2020/04/29/847732037/milk-delivery-business-booms-as-people-avoid-grocery-stores|title=Milk Delivery Business Booms As People Avoid Grocery Stores|website=NPR.org|date=April 29, 2020 |publisher=NPR|access-date=May 29, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231211/https://www.npr.org/2020/04/29/847732037/milk-delivery-business-booms-as-people-avoid-grocery-stores|url-status=live}}</ref>

On June 19, 2020, about a month after starting to reopen their outlets in the US, [[Apple Inc.|Apple]] temporarily closed 11 retail stores across [[COVID-19 pandemic in Arizona|Arizona]], [[COVID-19 pandemic in Florida|Florida]], [[COVID-19 pandemic in North Carolina|North Carolina]], and [[COVID-19 pandemic in South Carolina|South Carolina]] due to rising numbers of COVID-19 cases.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2020/06/19/business/stock-market-today-coronavirus.html|title=Stocks Fall as Apple Says It Will Close Stores Again|work=[[The New York Times]]|date=June 19, 2020|access-date=June 21, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231309/https://www.nytimes.com/2020/06/19/business/stock-market-today-coronavirus.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

In July 2020, the largest supermarket chain in the country, [[Kroger]], stopped giving coins as change. Because the U.S. Mint had decreased coin production to protect its employees during the pandemic, and because banks were giving fewer coins to the Federal Reserve, Kroger had difficulty obtaining enough coins for its business. (Instead, Kroger offered to make charitable donations or to issue store credit to the customer.)<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kroger, America's largest grocer, has stopped giving coins as change|date=July 14, 2020 |url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/americas-largest-grocer-stopped-giving-202100224.html|access-date=July 15, 2020|publisher=Yahoo! Finance|language=en-US|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231204/https://finance.yahoo.com/news/americas-largest-grocer-stopped-giving-202100224.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

On June 13, 2021, the [[Washington Prime Group]], an owner of over 100 U.S. shopping malls, filed for bankruptcy.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Valinsky|first=Jordan|date=14 June 2021|title=Major US mall owner files for bankruptcy|url=https://www.cnn.com/2021/06/14/business/washington-prime-group-mall-bankruptcy/index.html|access-date=15 June 2021|website=CNN}}</ref>

=== Seafood industry ===
The COVID-19 pandemic affected fishing and seafood industries worldwide beginning with the temporary lockdown and closing of all but essential businesses. This affected restaurants, which are where 80% of fish and seafood consumption takes place in the U.S.<ref name="latimes20200320">{{Cite web|date=March 20, 2020|title=Environmental groups urge Americans to eat more fish while hunkering down against virus|url=https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-03-20/environmental-groups-support-local-fishermen-during-coronavirus-pandemic|access-date=May 11, 2020|website=Los Angeles Times|language=en-US|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231229/https://www.latimes.com/california/story/2020-03-20/environmental-groups-support-local-fishermen-during-coronavirus-pandemic|url-status=live}}</ref> Disruption to seafood exports resulted in cancelled orders to both national and overseas purchases.<ref>{{Cite web|date=March 22, 2020|title=Coronavirus devastates Bowen's fishing industry, farmers face uncertain winter – ABC News|url=https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-03-23/coronavirus-downturn-wreaks-havoc-in-bowen-coral-trout-industry/12076550|access-date=May 11, 2020|publisher=Australian Broadcasting Corporation|language=en-AU|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231215/https://www.abc.net.au/news/2020-03-23/coronavirus-downturn-wreaks-havoc-in-bowen-coral-trout-industry/12076550|url-status=live}}</ref> Many fisheries became unprofitable and seafood export and import outlets were shuttered, with fishing crews and seafood processing workers being driven into finding other work as market dries up.<ref>{{Cite news|first=Damian|last=Carrington|date=March 18, 2020|title=Coronavirus: 'severe shock' to UK fishing as markets dry up|language=en-GB|work=The Guardian|url=https://www.theguardian.com/business/2020/mar/18/coronavirus-severe-shock-to-uk-fishing-as-markets-dry-up|access-date=May 11, 2020|issn=0261-3077|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231209/https://www.theguardian.com/business/2020/mar/18/coronavirus-severe-shock-to-uk-fishing-as-markets-dry-up|url-status=live}}</ref>

President Trump also issued an executive order proposing U.S. farm-raised fish and shellfish as the new suitable form of seafood consumption in the United States. The executive order was meant to "encourage public-private partnerships and promote inter-agency, intergovernmental, and international cooperation in order to improve global maritime domain awareness… and economic growth,"<ref>{{Cite web|title=Executive Order on Promoting American Seafood Competitiveness and Economic Growth|url=https://trumpwhitehouse.archives.gov/presidential-actions/executive-order-promoting-american-seafood-competitiveness-economic-growth/|access-date=May 12, 2020|via=[[NARA|National Archives]]|publisher=[[White House]]|language=en-US}}</ref> as well as strengthening national export and decreasing foreign competition.<ref>{{Cite news|author-last2=Navarro|author-first1=Joe |author-last1=Grogan |author-first2= Peter|date=May 7, 2020|title=Opinion {{!}} Trump Lifts the Net off American Fishing|language=en-US|work=The Wall Street Journal|url=https://www.wsj.com/articles/trump-lifts-the-net-off-american-fishing-11588875561|access-date=May 12, 2020|issn=0099-9660|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231211/https://www.wsj.com/articles/trump-lifts-the-net-off-american-fishing-11588875561|url-status=live}}</ref>

The pandemic impacted seafood supply chains on every level, from fisherfolk and coastal communities to large scale processors, distributors, foodservice buyers, and consumers.<ref name="CanadaMobility">{{Cite journal|last1=Neis|first1=Barbara|last2=Neil|first2=Kerri|last3=Lippel|first3=Katherine|date=April 21, 2020|title=Mobility in a Pandemic: COVID-19 and the Mobile Labour Force|url=https://www.onthemovepartnership.ca/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/COVID-and-Mobile-Labour-Force-Working-Paper.pdf|journal=Working Paper|via=On the Move Partnership|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231054/https://www.onthemovepartnership.ca/wp-content/uploads/2020/04/COVID-and-Mobile-Labour-Force-Working-Paper.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref>{{page needed|date=May 2020}}<ref>{{Cite web|date=March 17, 2020|title=Coronavirus impact on seafood sector 'massive'|url=https://www.fishfarmermagazine.com/news/coronavirus-impact-on-seafood-sector-massive/|access-date=May 10, 2020|website=Fish Farmer Magazine|language=en-GB|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231440/https://www.fishfarmermagazine.com/news/coronavirus-impact-on-seafood-sector-massive/|url-status=live}}</ref> Many seafood commodities suffered from unprecedented drops in market value and the communities that capture and produce these commodities were significantly disrupted.<ref name="CanadaMobility" />{{page needed|date=May 2020}}<ref>{{Cite web|title=Coronavirus-related closures impacting US fisheries, driving down prices|url=https://www.seafoodsource.com/news/supply-trade/covid-19-related-closures-impacting-us-fisheries-driving-down-prices|access-date=May 10, 2020|website=seafoodsource.com|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231217/https://www.seafoodsource.com/news/supply-trade/covid-19-related-closures-impacting-us-fisheries-driving-down-prices|url-status=live}}</ref> A variety of policy approaches balancing public and economic health were implemented in response. On May 7, 2020, the US Secretary of Commerce announced an amendment to Sec. 12005 of the [[CARES Act|COVID-19 Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act]] allocating $300&nbsp;million in financial assistance to COVID-19 affected coastal and fishery communities.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Fisheries|first=NOAA|date=May 7, 2020|title=President Signs New Executive Order Promoting American Seafood Competitiveness and Economic Growth {{!}} NOAA Fisheries|url=https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/leadership-message/president-signs-new-executive-order-promoting-american-seafood-competitiveness-and|access-date=May 10, 2020|website=NOAA|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231214/https://www.fisheries.noaa.gov/leadership-message/president-signs-new-executive-order-promoting-american-seafood-competitiveness-and|url-status=live}}</ref> On the same day, President Trump enacted the Executive Order on Promoting American Seafood Competitiveness and Economic Growth. The order aims to increase the competitiveness of the American seafood industry via deregulation. Under the order, all eight [[U.S. Regional Fishery Management Councils|Regional Fishery Management Councils]] must provide policy recommendations to "reduce burdens on domestic fishing" and initiate these recommendations by May 7, 2021.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Executive Order on Promoting American Seafood Competitiveness and Economic Growth|url=https://trumpwhitehouse.archives.gov/presidential-actions/executive-order-promoting-american-seafood-competitiveness-economic-growth/|access-date=May 10, 2020|via=[[NARA|National Archives]]|publisher=[[White House]]|language=en-US}}</ref>
=== Shipping and mail ===

With consumers increasingly relying on online retailers, [[Amazon (company)|Amazon]] planned to hire another 100,000 warehouse and delivery workers and raise wages $2 per hour through April 2020. They also reported shortages of certain household staples.<ref>[https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/16/amazon-to-hire-100000-warehouse-and-delivery-workers.html Amazon to hire 100,000 more workers and give raises to current staff to deal with coronavirus demands] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231426/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/16/amazon-to-hire-100000-warehouse-and-delivery-workers.html |date=February 14, 2021 }} by Annie Palmer, CNBC, March 16, 2020</ref> At its warehouses, Amazon stopped exit screenings, as well as group meetings at the beginning of shifts, and staggered shift times and break times. The company also announced it would provide up to two weeks of pay to all employees diagnosed with COVID-19 or placed into quarantine, but presumably not for employees who merely had symptoms of fever and cough.<ref>[https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/21/jeff-bezos-memo-about-coronavirus-safety-at-amazon-warehouses.html?&qsearchterm=Read%20the%20memo%20Jeff%20Bezos%20sent%20to%20Amazon%20employees%20about%20coronavirus%20safety%20at%20warehouses Read the memo Jeff Bezos sent to Amazon employees about coronavirus safety at warehouses] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231234/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/21/jeff-bezos-memo-about-coronavirus-safety-at-amazon-warehouses.html?qsearchterm=Read+the+memo+Jeff+Bezos+sent+to+Amazon+employees+about+coronavirus+safety+at+warehouses |date=February 14, 2021 }}, CNBC, updated March 22, 2020.</ref> Amazon workers complained paid medical leave was difficult to obtain because of limited access to COVID-19 testing, and some petitioned the company to extend paid leave to elderly and medically vulnerable workers without a positive test.<ref name="kentucky">{{cite web|title=Amazon Closes Kentucky Warehouse After Workers Test Positive|website=NPR|date=March 26, 2020|url=https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/03/26/822045229/amazon-closes-kentucky-warehouse-after-workers-test-positive|access-date=April 1, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231219/https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/03/26/822045229/amazon-closes-kentucky-warehouse-after-workers-test-positive|url-status=live|last1=Selyukh|first1=Alina}}</ref> As small numbers of workers tested positive for COVID-19, various Amazon warehouses closed for sanitization, including one in Kentucky for several days.<ref name="kentucky" /> Amazon workers at the [[Staten Island]] warehouse and some [[Instacart]] workers nationwide separately announced strikes for March 30, demanding access to PPE, better sick pay, hazard pay for Instacart orders, and a longer closure of the Staten Island warehouse for cleaning.<ref>{{cite web|last1=Selyukh |first1=Alina |title=Amazon, Instacart Grocery Delivery Workers Demand Coronavirus Protection And Pay|website=NPR.org|date=March 30, 2020 |url=https://www.npr.org/2020/03/30/823767492/amazon-instacart-grocery-delivery-workers-strike-for-coronavirus-protection-and-|access-date=April 1, 2020|publisher=NPR|archive-date=March 30, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200330150146/https://www.npr.org/2020/03/30/823767492/amazon-instacart-grocery-delivery-workers-strike-for-coronavirus-protection-and-|url-status=live}}</ref> From March 1 to September 19, 2020, nearly 20,000 of Amazon's U.S. employees tested positive or were presumed positive.<ref>{{Cite web|last=O'Brien|first=Sara Ashley|date=October 2, 2020|title=Amazon said nearly 20,000 workers got coronavirus|url=https://www.cnn.com/2020/10/01/tech/amazon-covid-cases/index.html|access-date=October 3, 2020|publisher=CNN|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231442/https://www.cnn.com/2020/10/01/tech/amazon-covid-cases/index.html|url-status=live}}</ref>

The ''[[Chicago Tribune]]'' reported in March 2020 that employees at [[United Parcel Service|UPS]], [[FedEx]], and [[XPO, Inc.]] were often pressured not to take time off, even with symptoms such as fever and cough consistent with COVID-19. Public health authorities stated the risk was relatively low to customers receiving packages, in part because COVID-19 does not live for very long on cardboard, but infection most certainly was a danger for employees working beside crowded conveyor belts.<ref>[https://www.chicagotribune.com/coronavirus/ct-nw-nyt-coronavirus-package-delivery-employees-sick-20200321-kpijftkmlba3zodkmvtmhxojs4-story.html 'Terrified' package delivery employees are going to work sick] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231236/https://www.chicagotribune.com/coronavirus/ct-nw-nyt-coronavirus-package-delivery-employees-sick-20200321-kpijftkmlba3zodkmvtmhxojs4-story.html |date=February 14, 2021 }}, Chicago Tribune (New York Times), March 21, 2020.</ref>

UPS and FedEx suspended all delivery time guarantees but continued domestic pickups and deliveries. Some international private package deliveries were delayed, while others were completely suspended due to government shutdowns in other countries.<ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.fedex.com/content/dam/fedex/us-united-states/Service-Alerts/images/2020/Q4/2019_nCoV_Service_Impacts_May_12.pdf |title=Service Impact of COVID-19 |date=May 12, 2020 |access-date=May 14, 2020 |archive-date=February 14, 2021 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231112/https://www.fedex.com/content/dam/fedex/us-united-states/Service-Alerts/images/2020/Q4/2019_nCoV_Service_Impacts_May_12.pdf |url-status=live }}</ref>

Domestic package delivery continued, with two- and three-day package delivery guarantees extended by one day, due to lack of affordable domestic cargo transportation (presumably due to cancellation of most passenger airline flights).<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/pdf/expected-delivery-changes-april-17.pdf|title=Expected Delivery Changes for Priority Mail and First-Class Package Services|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231115/https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/pdf/expected-delivery-changes-april-17.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> A large amount of mail was held due to sustained business shutdowns, with the USPS extending the time period before returning to sender.<ref>{{cite web|title=USPS Coronavirus Updates for Residential Customers|url=https://faq.usps.com/s/article/USPS-Coronavirus-Updates-for-Residential-Customers|access-date=May 14, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231222/https://faq.usps.com/s/article/USPS-Coronavirus-Updates-for-Residential-Customers|url-status=live}}</ref>
The USPS stopped accepting international mail for many countries, either due to suspension of service in the destination country or lack of affordable transportation.<ref name="intl_may14">{{cite web|date=May 14, 2020|title=International service disruptions|url=https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/international/welcome.htm|access-date=May 14, 2020|archive-date=September 8, 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200908220258/https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/international/welcome.htm|url-status=live}}</ref> Some military and diplomatic mail destinations were also shut down.<ref name="intl_may14" />
Some mail destined for Europe was diverted from air to sea transport, with a small number of containers sent by ships departing from the JFK International Service Center in New York for Rotterdam on April 20<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/pdf/air-to-sea-diversion-in-effect.pdf|title=International Service Impact / Alternate Transportation: Air to Sea Diversion in Effect|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231116/https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/pdf/air-to-sea-diversion-in-effect.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> and April 27,<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/pdf/second-air-to-sea-diversion-in-effect.pdf|title=International Service Alert / Alternate Transport: Second Air to Sea Diversion in Effect|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231116/https://about.usps.com/newsroom/service-alerts/pdf/second-air-to-sea-diversion-in-effect.pdf|url-status=live}}</ref> 2020.

Some postal workers complained of lack of social distancing at work and outages of supplies, including masks, gloves, and rubber bands.<ref>{{Cite web|last1=Meloni|first1=Robert|last2=Carrier|first2=Letter|title=Postal Workers Say They Lack Supplies, Training To Protect Themselves From Virus|website=NPR.org|date=March 24, 2020 |url=https://www.npr.org/2020/03/24/820330579/postal-workers-say-they-lack-supplies-training-to-protect-themselves-from-virus|publisher=NPR|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231414/https://www.npr.org/2020/03/24/820330579/postal-workers-say-they-lack-supplies-training-to-protect-themselves-from-virus|url-status=live}}</ref>

In response to the workplace hazards associated with handling and delivering [[Reverse logistics|returned packages]], some companies instituted policies meant to minimize risk to both workers and customers. Examples include extending the amount of time allotted to customers to return an item,<ref>{{Cite web|last=Thomas|first=Lauren|date=April 14, 2020|title=Retailers face another challenge during coronavirus: Handling returns|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/04/14/coronavirus-dealing-with-returns-could-be-bigger-burden-for-retailers.html|access-date=August 20, 2020|publisher=CNBC|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231444/https://www.cnbc.com/2020/04/14/coronavirus-dealing-with-returns-could-be-bigger-burden-for-retailers.html|url-status=live}}</ref> allowing customers to return an item to a physical location even if they purchased it online,<ref>{{Cite web|title=Kohl's Now Accepts Amazon Returns at All Stores|url=http://corporate.kohls.com/news/archive-/2019/july/kohl-s-now-accepts-amazon-returns-at-all-stores-|access-date=August 20, 2020|website=Kohls Corporation|language=en|archive-date=February 14, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231253/https://corporate.kohls.com/news/archive-/2019/july/kohl-s-now-accepts-amazon-returns-at-all-stores-|url-status=live}}</ref> and quarantining returned packages for at least one day upon arrival in the warehouse.

==== Financial impact on the U.S. Postal Service ====
Ultimately, the pandemic contributed to an increase in e-commerce. By mid-April 2020, with many businesses dropping advertising campaigns or closing completely, the [[United States Postal Service]] (USPS) experienced a 30% drop in volume<ref name="save_usps">{{Cite web|last=Horn|first=Austin|date=14 April 2020|title=Hoping To Save The Postal Service, People Rush To Buy Stamps|url=https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/04/14/834336341/hoping-to-save-the-postal-service-people-rush-to-buy-stamps|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231301/https://www.npr.org/sections/coronavirus-live-updates/2020/04/14/834336341/hoping-to-save-the-postal-service-people-rush-to-buy-stamps|archive-date=February 14, 2021|access-date=May 27, 2020|publisher=NPR}}</ref> and projected a $13&nbsp;billion loss in revenue<ref>{{Cite web|title=U.S. Postal Service Stands To Suffer Huge Losses From Coronavirus Pandemic|url=https://www.wbur.org/hereandnow/2020/04/21/us-postal-service-losing-money|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231254/https://www.wbur.org/hereandnow/2020/04/21/us-postal-service-losing-money|archive-date=February 14, 2021|access-date=May 27, 2020|website=wbur.org|date=April 21, 2020 }}</ref> for 2020. The service was reluctant to accept a $10 billion loan allocated by the [[CARES Act]] because it would increase already high debt levels and give too much control to the [[United States Department of the Treasury|U.S. Treasury]], possibly advancing the Trump administration's plan to privatize the postal service.<ref name="npr_usps">{{Cite web|last1=Naylor |first1=Brian |title=You've Got Less Mail: The Postal Service Is Suffering Amid The Coronavirus|website=NPR.org|date=April 8, 2020 |url=https://www.npr.org/2020/04/08/828949609/youve-got-less-mail-the-postal-service-is-suffering-amid-the-coronavirus|url-status=live|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210214231236/https://www.npr.org/2020/04/08/828949609/youve-got-less-mail-the-postal-service-is-suffering-amid-the-coronavirus|archive-date=February 14, 2021|access-date=May 27, 2020|publisher=NPR}}</ref> It requested $89&nbsp;billion in grant aid from Congress.<ref name="save_usps" /> The initially projected 2020 losses did not occur. In the fiscal year 2020, compared to the previous year, the amount of mail sent by the USPS grew 18.8 percent and operating revenue increased nearly $2 billion.<ref>{{Cite web|date=13 November 2020|title=U.S. Postal Service Reports Fiscal Year 2020 Results - Newsroom - About.usps.com|url=https://about.usps.com/newsroom/national-releases/2020/1113-usps-reports-fiscal-year-2020-results.htm|access-date=2021-10-09|website=about.usps.com}}</ref>

=== Travel industry ===
[[File:Delta Planes at MCI (49879371996) (cropped).jpg|thumb|[[Delta Air Lines]] planes parked on a taxiway at [[Kansas City International Airport]]. The planes are parked due to the sharp decrease in demand for air travel.|alt=Many in line airplanes with the Delta Air Lines logo on the tail, parked on pavement behind a fence.]]
[[File:President Trump and the Vice President meet with Airline CEOs about the Coronavirus (49618513938).jpg|thumb|March 4: President Trump and Airline CEOs discuss COVID-19's impact on the [[Air transportation in the United States|travel industry]].]]
[[File:U.S. motor gasoline product supplied (49784554532).png|thumb|Demand for motor gasoline fell sharply.]]

In mid-March, most major American and foreign airlines began cutting back on domestic and international flights as a result of the sudden drop in travel demand from the pandemic and subsequent travel bans, phasing out some routes entirely.<ref name="nyt-airlines">[https://www.nytimes.com/2020/03/16/travel/international-airlines-service-cuts-coronavirus.html "Airlines Cut International Flights in Response to the Pandemic"], ''The New York Times'', March 17, 2020</ref><ref>[https://www.washingtonpost.com/business/2020/03/12/delta-command-center-coronavirus/?arc404=true "Inside Delta's command center the week the coronavirus devastated the airline industry"], ''The Washington Post'', March 13, 2020</ref> Cruise lines suspended all departures from the United States on March 14.<ref>{{cite web|title=CLIA Announces Voluntary Suspension in U.S. Cruise Operations|url=https://cruising.org/news-and-research/press-room/2020/march/clia-covid-19-toolkit|website=Cruise Line Industry Association|access-date=May 27, 2020|archive-date=April 2, 2021|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210402172117/https://cruising.org/en/news-and-research/press-room/2020/april/clia-covid-19-toolkit}}</ref>

The outbreak produced occasional disruptions to [[air traffic control]] with [[area control center]]s in New York and Indianapolis, and airport towers at [[Midway International Airport]] in Chicago and [[McCarran International Airport]] in Las Vegas evacuated for sterilization after at least one person who had been in each tested positive for COVID-19.<ref>{{cite web|last=Walker|first=Stephen|title=America's Air Traffic Control System Is Suffering Crippling Shutdowns Due To COVID-19|url=https://www.thedrive.com/the-war-zone/32680/americas-air-traffic-control-system-is-suffering-crippling-shutdowns-due-to-covid-19|access-date=March 24, 2020|website=The Drive |date=March 21, 2020 }}</ref>

On March 14, [[Amtrak]] reduced its service between Washington and Boston as the COVID-19 outbreak drastically decreased travel demand. It faced steep revenue losses during the crisis. It also asked noncritical employees "to take time off on an unpaid basis."<ref>[https://www.washingtonpost.com/transportation/2020/03/14/amtrak-cuts-more-trains-coronavirus-impacts-travel-demand/ "Amtrak cuts more trains as coronavirus lessens demand"], ''The Washington Post'', March 14, 2020</ref> By the following week, New York's subways, usually the nation's busiest, were running mostly empty, which had the [[Metropolitan Transportation Authority]] using $1&nbsp;billion from its line of credit to stay afloat.<ref>[https://www.washingtonpost.com/local/trafficandcommuting/in-a-week-the-coronavirus-razed-us-transit-and-rail-systems/2020/03/22/ca58a88a-6a21-11ea-9923-57073adce27c_story.html "In a week, the coronavirus razed U.S. transit and rail systems"], ''The Washington Post'', March 22, 2020</ref>

The lobbying group for the airline industry, [[Airlines for America]] (A4A), on March 16 called for a $50&nbsp;billion subsidy, including $4&nbsp;billion for cargo services.<ref>{{cite web|last=Gangitano|first=Alex|date=March 16, 2020|title=Airline industry calls for $50B in assistance|url=https://thehill.com/business-a-lobbying/business-a-lobbying/487820-airline-industry-calls-for-25b-in-assistance-for|access-date=April 5, 2020|website=The Hill|language=en}}</ref> [[CNBC]] reported that airlines were preparing for a ban on domestic flights after President Trump said on March 14 he was considering travel curbs and acting DHS Secretary [[Chad Wolf]] said all options remained on the table when asked about a possible ban, the first since the [[September 11 attacks]] in 2001. United Airlines said they expected a drop of $1.5&nbsp;billion in March revenue, American Airlines said they expected to decrease domestic capacity by 20% in April and 30% in May, and Delta Air Lines told employees it would cut capacity by 40%.<ref>{{cite web|last=Josephs|first=Leslie|date=March 16, 2020|title=Coronavirus forces airlines to consider a once unthinkable possibility—halting US flights|url=https://www.cnbc.com/2020/03/16/coronavirus-makes-airlines-consider-chances-for-a-halt-to-us-flights.html|access-date=April 5, 2020|publisher=CNBC|language=en}}</ref>

===Transport and transport safety===

Covid has had many effects on transport: reduction of traffic, increase of speed, increase of extreme speeding, increase in commercial shipping activity, increase dependence as a commercial vehicle society, increase working hours for truckers, and increase death rates per motor vehicle accident in the United States.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://pandmllp.com/blog/2020/07/traffic-accident-fatalities-have-increased-during-the-quarantine-caused-by-covid-19/|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200810072753/https://pandmllp.com/blog/2020/07/traffic-accident-fatalities-have-increased-during-the-quarantine-caused-by-covid-19/|title=Traffic accident fatalities have increased during the quarantine caused by COVID-19|work=Paranjpe & Mahadass LLP|date=July 27, 2020|archive-date=August 10, 2020}}</ref> The [[History of the trucking industry in the United States|freight industry]] experienced a shortage of truck drivers,<ref>{{cite web |title=The driver shortage: A big piece of the tight capacity puzzle |url=https://www.transportdive.com/news/coronavirus-truck-driver-shortage-capacity-Q3-2020/584788/ |website=Transport Dive |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20200930190920/https://www.transportdive.com/news/coronavirus-truck-driver-shortage-capacity-Q3-2020/584788/ |archive-date=September 30, 2020 |url-status=live}}</ref> and freight rates rose higher than 2019 levels.<ref>{{cite web |title=Truck vs. train: Which has the upper hand as spot rates soar? |url=https://www.transportdive.com/news/trucking-spot-rates-tight-capacity-rail-intermodal/586586/ |website=Transport Dive |archive-url= https://web.archive.org/web/20201015191341/https://www.transportdive.com/news/trucking-spot-rates-tight-capacity-rail-intermodal/586586/ |archive-date=October 15, 2020 |date=October 15, 2020 |url-status=live}}</ref> Supply problems and sudden demand for socially-distanced recreation and alternatives to public transport caused a shortage of bicycles.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.wgbh.org/news/national-news/2020/06/19/bicycling-boom-keeping-up-with-pandemic-fueled-demand|title=Bicycling Boom: Keeping Up With Pandemic-Fueled Demand|date=June 19, 2020|website=News}}</ref>
===Commercial real estate===
{{main|2020s commercial real estate distress}}

The pandemic reduced the value of office properties in New York City by as much as $28 billion.<ref name="bloomberg_nyc_0921">{{cite news |last1=Wong |first1=Natalie |title=Pandemic's Toll on NYC Office Market Will Last Years, State Says |url=https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2021-10-07/pandemic-s-toll-on-nyc-office-market-will-last-years-state-says?sref=CIpmV6x8 |access-date=1 May 2023 |work=Bloomberg |date=7 October 2021}}</ref>

=== Pandemic-related fraud ===
The vast increase in the flow of federal funds to American citizens (via the [[CARES Act]] and [[Paycheck Protection Program|related measures]]) resulted in a corresponding increase in benefits fraud, both from businesses and individuals.<ref name=":7">{{Cite web |last=Hughlett |first=Mike |date=2025-03-29 |title=As COVID spread, so did fraud. Minnesota saw a lot of it. |url=https://www.startribune.com/as-covid-spread-so-did-fraud-minnesota-saw-a-lot-of-it/601244557 |access-date=2025-03-29 |website=[[Minnesota Star Tribune]] |language=en}}</ref> In the largest individual case, [[Feeding Our Future]], dozens of individuals conspired to receive nearly $250 million in [[United States Department of Agriculture|USDA]] child nutrition funds while failing to actually provide meals to children. Nationwide, as of 2025, more than 4200 Americans had been federally charged with pandemic fraud, leading to more than 2400 convictions and $2 billion in recovered funds and forfeitures.<ref name=":7" />

==Impact on federal budget deficit and debt==
{{Expand section|date=July 2021}}
Due to the [[COVID-19 pandemic in the United States|COVID-19 pandemic]], Congress and President Trump enacted the $2.2&nbsp;trillion [[Coronavirus Aid, Relief, and Economic Security Act]] (CARES) on March 18, 2020. The [[Congressional Budget Office]] estimated that the budget deficit for fiscal year 2020 would increase to $3.3&nbsp;trillion or 16% GDP, more than triple that of 2019 and the largest deficit since 1945 (measured as percentage of GDP). CBO also forecast the debt held by the public would rise to 98% GDP in 2020, compared with 79% in 2019 and 35% in 2007 before the [[Great Recession]].<ref name="CBO_Aug2020">{{Cite web |date=September 2, 2020 |title=An update to the budget outlook 2020 to 2030 |url=https://www.cbo.gov/publication/56517 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200906093315/https://www.cbo.gov/publication/56517 |archive-date=September 6, 2020 |access-date=September 6, 2020}}</ref> 

Furthermore, the Federal Reserve (Fed) used both [[monetary policy]] and its [[Lender of last resort|lender of the last resort]] role to prevent the economy falling into a deeper recession. In March 2020, the [[federal funds rate]] was lowered by a total of 1.5 points, resulting in a rate of 0% to 0.25%<ref>{{Cite web |title=What did the Fed do in response to the COVID-19 crisis? |url=https://www.brookings.edu/articles/fed-response-to-covid19/ |access-date=2026-04-07 |website=Brookings |language=en-US}}</ref>. By reducing the funds rate, banks are able to borrow funds overnight from other banks with lower cost. The Fed also gave foreign banks access to borrow from the discount window which they were not been able to before. Several emergency lending facilities such as the [[Primary Dealer Credit Facility]] (PDCF) and the Money Market Mutual Fund Liquidity Facility (MMLF) were created or relaunched to provide short-term loans to firms.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Federal Reserve’s Response to COVID-19: Policy Issues |url=https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R46411 |access-date=2026-04-07 |website=www.congress.gov}}</ref> [[Term Asset-Backed Securities Loan Facility|Term-Asset Backed Securities Loan Facility]] (TALF) lent $4.1 billion in asset-backed securities (ABS) to financially support households, consumers, and businesses.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The Federal Reserve’s Response to COVID-19: Policy Issues |url=https://www.congress.gov/crs-product/R46411 |access-date=2026-04-07 |website=www.congress.gov}}</ref> The severity of the COVID-19 crisis forced the Fed to further increase liquidity by purchasing $500 billions of Treasuries and $200 billion worth of [[Mortgage-backed security|Mortgage Backed Securities (MBS)]], equivalent to 3.3% of the U.S. GDP in 2020.<ref>{{Cite journal |last=Occhino |first=Filippo |date=November 2023 |title=Quantitative Easing and Direct Lending in Response to the COVID-19 Crisis |url= |journal=[[Journal of Money, Credit and Banking]]}}</ref> An estimated of 4.70 trillion was spent on asset purchases, liquidity measures, lending facilities, and other loan purchase programs. <ref>{{Cite web |title=Covid Money Tracker |url=https://www.covidmoneytracker.org/ |access-date=2026-04-07 |website=www.covidmoneytracker.org |language=en}}</ref>

Under the current laws, CBO projects that debt will increase from 98% currently to 110% by 2032. Net interest rates will double from 1.6% to 3.2% by 2032.<ref>{{Cite web |title=The COVID Pandemic and the Federal Budget |url=https://www.brookings.edu/articles/the-covid-pandemic-and-the-federal-budget/ |access-date=2026-04-07 |website=Brookings |language=en-US}}</ref> 

Additional proposals for large stimulus packages in late 2020 were stalled.<ref>{{Cite news|last1=Kim|first1=Seung Min|last2=Stein|first2=Jeff|last3=DeBonis|first3=Mike|date=December 1, 2020|title=Bipartisan group of senators prepares $908 billion stimulus plan, aiming to break partisan logjam|newspaper=The Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/us-policy/2020/12/01/stimulus-congress-coronavirus/|access-date=December 1, 2020}}</ref> In December 2020, the [[Groundwork Collaborative]] think tank said a $3–4.5&nbsp;trillion stimulus package would be needed to return the economy to pre-pandemic levels.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Tsekova|first=Denitsa|date=December 12, 2020|title=U.S. needs estimated $4.5 trillion in stimulus to recover, analysis argues|url=https://finance.yahoo.com/news/us-needs-45-trillion-in-stimulus-to-recover-133548317.html|access-date=December 12, 2020|publisher=Yahoo! Finance|language=en-US}}</ref>

On July 1, 2021, the Congressional Budget Office said the federal deficit would reach $3 trillion for the second year in a row.<ref>{{Cite news|last=Stein|first=Jeff|date=1 July 2021|title=U.S. deficit to hit $3 trillion in 2021, then fade as stimulus relief expires, CBO says|newspaper=Washington Post|url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/us-policy/2021/07/01/cbo-deficit-biden-stimulus/|access-date=1 July 2021}}</ref>
The national debt of the United States as of January 2022 reached $30 trilion.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2022/02/01/us/politics/national-debt-30-trillion.html|title=U.S. National Debt Tops $30 Trillion as Borrowing Surged Amid Pandemic|first=Alan|last=Rappeport|newspaper=The New York Times|date=February 1, 2022}}</ref>
Gross Domestic Product grew by between 5% and 6% in year 2021 and between 4% and 5% in the start of year 2022.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.imf.org/en/Publications/WEO/Issues/2021/01/26/2021-world-economic-outlook-update|title=World Economic Outlook Update, January 2021: Policy Support and Vaccines Expected to Lift Activity|website=IMF}}</ref>

== Corporate bankruptcies ==
The following list is a selection of American companies that filed for bankruptcy during the pandemic.

! Sector !! data-sort-type="date" | Date of filing || Notes
